Brookline School Committee Meeting - June 18, 2026

School Committee · Brookline · 2026-06-18
tap to load the tape · nothing plays until you do
AI summary — supplements the official record

During the Brookline School Committee Meeting on June 18, 2026, a retirement celebration for several members of the school community, including Elizabeth Hendrickson, was acknowledged, with performance recognition and expressions of gratitude for their service, particularly regarding the METCO program [17:16][127:35]. The meeting also included discussions about future class sizes, equity in education, and plans for the upcoming school year, emphasizing the importance of maintaining support for low-income students and addressing disparities [119:15][148:01]. The committee highlighted the need for initiatives to enhance the educational experience and foster diversity in schools, with a focus on upcoming projects [198:01][259:23].

34:01 thank you so much for giving me the

34:05 Yeah.

34:06 [applause]

34:11 Thank you, Deborah. Elizabeth Gorman.

34:18 [applause]

34:26 Liz Gorman.

34:29 All right.

34:30 The microphone.

34:32 Um so, thank you to all of my people, my

34:35 home family,

34:36 my World Language Wellness family, you

34:39 are everything. Um thank you to the town

34:41 of Brookline for creating such an

34:43 enriching professional home for me for

34:45 all these years.

34:46 Thanks to Bob Weintrob and Manuela

34:48 Bartolomeo for taking a chance on me and

34:50 hiring me 30 years ago.

34:53 Um so what does one say about 30 years

34:55 of teaching in several minutes?

34:58 Uh one surprise I thought about like

35:00 what what surprised me.

35:02 Um one surprise is that it has not

35:05 gotten easier.

35:06 [laughter]

35:08 I have more skills, but I still work

35:11 long hours. I never feel caught up. Um

35:13 my husband can can confirm.

35:16 Uh sadly, I still get filled with dread

35:18 on Sunday nights and after vacations. Um

35:22 we are on every day, all day.

35:25 Uh I do long for a job that I don't

35:27 bring home every day and every weekend.

35:30 Uh but the stress dissipates every

35:33 Monday morning as soon as I'm in my

35:35 classroom with students.

35:38 It's a very happy place for me and it

35:40 sounds like for a lot of you.

35:42 Um another surprise is that it is just

35:44 as fun and ex- as exciting as it was 30

35:47 years ago. So that's a good surprise.

35:51 Um I'm not a teacher who will be

35:52 remembered for like some single great

35:54 achievement, though I am proud of a few

35:56 things that I helped build.

35:58 Uh I helped build a China exchange, the

36:01 climbing wall,

36:03 a network of mindfulness work with

36:04 teachers, loads of Spanish curriculum

36:07 units. Uh but mostly my teaching, like

36:10 most teachers, which to clarify is not

36:13 over,

36:13 [laughter]

36:14 um has been defined by a lot of effort

36:17 and made of a million small moments of

36:20 joy and kindness.

36:22 Um it's not a thankless job.

36:24 In addition to the never-boring daily

36:26 delights, Every year around this time

36:29 the thank you cards come in. I keep a

36:31 physical file folder

36:33 labeled nice.

36:35 [laughter]

36:36 For the particularly heartfelt ones, it

36:38 is getting nice and fat. Um this note

36:41 though I too brought I'm going to share

36:43 us some student words. Uh this note that

36:45 I will read to you arrived unexpect-

36:48 unexpectedly in October of this year and

36:51 um

36:52 embodies what I'm most proud of.

36:54 Uh and I'm going to change the name for

36:55 privacy. So it said this email came in.

36:58 I opened my box and said, "Hi Ms.

37:00 Gorman. I'm Emma and I had you for my

37:02 junior year Spanish class about 4 years

37:04 ago. I just wanted to reach out and say

37:06 thank you. During my junior year of high

37:08 school I struggled with my mental health

37:11 and you honestly saved my life.

37:14 You asked me to stay after class one day

37:16 and asked me why I was missing so many

37:18 classes. It didn't feel confrontational

37:21 at all. Instead it felt like you

37:23 genuinely cared and we began to talk

37:25 regularly. Having one person care about

37:28 me during that time

37:30 was what I needed to stay alive.

37:33 Now I'm a junior in college in

37:34 Philadelphia and I'm studying to become

37:37 an elementary school teacher.

37:39 [laughter]

37:41 If I become half the teacher and person

37:43 you are I will feel successful. Thank

37:45 you so much for having a positive impact

37:47 on my life especially when I needed it

37:50 the most. I hope this email reaches you

37:52 and I hope you have a great school year.

37:54 Best, Emma.

37:55 Um every teacher makes a difference and

37:57 we often don't know when.

38:00 Please keep trust to the school

38:02 committee. Please keep trusting and

38:03 supporting teachers

38:05 to be great, to follow their passions,

38:07 to create the conditions for them to be

38:09 able to show up healthy for others, and

38:12 please keep our class sizes small

38:15 [laughter]

38:16 so we can know every human in our rooms.

38:19 I am so excited that Emma will soon join

38:21 the ranks of future teachers. Thank you

38:24 and congratulations to you all.

38:29 [applause]

38:33 Thank you, Liz. Michelle Greeley.

38:41 [applause]

38:44 Thank you so much, school committee.

38:45 This is such an honor. It's also an

38:48 honor, but yet a daunting realization

38:50 that my gray hairs are coming in at a

38:52 much faster pace right now.

38:55 As a BEEP teacher, I've taught at FRR,

38:57 the Lynch Center, Driscoll, Lincoln, and

39:00 I'm currently teaching pre-K at BEEP at

39:02 Putterham.

39:04 My classroom, no matter where it stands,

39:06 is my home away from home.

39:09 I'd like to take you all on a field trip

39:11 there right now.

39:12 Now, for this trip, I'd like to you to

39:15 all humor me and close your eyes once I

39:17 count to three.

39:19 With your eyes shut, you'll be able to

39:21 envision all the sights as you listen to

39:24 four and five-year-olds describe the map

39:27 that leads to PKG.

39:29 1 2 3.

39:32 Now, close your eyes and imagine, for

39:35 this is BEEP at Putterham.

39:38 First, you ring the buzzer.

39:40 Then, you push one of the two doors. On

39:43 the other side is the door to the

39:45 playground. There are a lot of windows

39:47 there.

39:48 The stairs are in the middle.

39:50 Follow the arrows up the stairs. We are

39:53 going straight to near where the

39:54 elevator is.

39:56 Don't use that elevator if there's a

39:58 fire.

39:59 This is the glass door to go inside.

40:02 Now, I have to thank my dad for that

40:03 fire safety tip because he came in so

40:06 many years during his time as a

40:08 firefighter in Newton and shared with

40:11 all of my students his great fire safety

40:13 tips.

40:14 Now, here you are. This is the pictures

40:16 of all the friends at school. There are

40:18 all the teachers. All up on the side is

40:21 a long hallway. Now, the letters right

40:23 here means it's the classroom.

40:26 Abracadabra, everyone open your eyes.

40:29 We're here.

40:31 You have just landed in a corner of a

40:32 BEEP classroom where true magic happens.

40:36 The magic happens as the students here

40:38 learn from one another.

40:40 The joy happens as they celebrate and

40:42 praise one another.

40:44 Now, I would like you to listen to their

40:46 joy. These are their words.

40:50 I love your book so much and it's funny.

40:53 And thank you so much for making it.

40:55 It's so cute and I love it so much. I

40:58 love your story.

41:00 It is like a wow maze. I love your

41:03 pictures that you made. I love that you

41:06 can read. I love the letters that you

41:09 can do.

41:10 I very like your drawing. I love how

41:13 well you're reading. You are just doing

41:15 so great.

41:18 In BEEP, we never underestimate the

41:21 power of a word or a gesture.

41:23 The words that we choose help others to

41:25 feel loved, special, appreciated,

41:28 valued, and respected.

41:31 Just as significant as our words are

41:33 also the gestures of those who cannot

41:36 produce language.

41:38 Positive gestures, handshakes,

41:41 movements, mumbles, and eye glances

41:43 carry the same weight as our words.

41:46 Some of my best times in BEEP were

41:49 dancing with a nonverbal learner.

41:52 Her smiles and laughter were like word

41:55 lollipops, and that's the truth.

41:58 I came to Brookline because of the

42:00 school system's commitment to

42:01 acknowledging and celebrating diversity.

42:04 Although I've been all over the map of

42:07 Brookline, I know firsthand that

42:09 Brookline is the place to be for

42:11 inclusion and a place where we can all

42:13 together feel valued.

42:15 Thank you to everyone in Brookline and a

42:17 special thank you to Margaret, our

42:19 principal, Rachel and Aaron, and all of

42:22 the paraprofessionals and colleagues

42:24 that I've worked with.

42:26 I also want to thank my dad because he

42:28 instilled in me his love for maps.

42:31 Dad, you've always been my compass and I

42:34 thank you for always being there.

42:36 My dad was a Newton firefighter for 40

42:38 years. So, I'm going to be here for a

42:41 lot longer.

42:43 To my mom who taught me how to enlighten

42:45 children with creative and inspirational

42:47 projects and curriculum. As a preschool

42:50 teacher herself, she taught me patience,

42:52 compassion, and what it means to be a

42:54 dedicated teacher.

42:56 Thank you also to my daughters, Joella

42:58 and Julia,

42:59 who they share their they share their

43:01 mom, me, day and night with all of my

43:04 students and their families. They have

43:06 supported me in more ways than I can

43:08 ever begin to describe.

43:10 Thank you. Thank you.

43:13 [applause]

43:17 Thank you, Michelle. Lisa Lamont Harris.

43:24 [applause and cheering]

43:32 All right.

43:33 Um so, how do you distill 30 years into

43:36 3 minutes?

43:38 Um bullets. So, I'm going to speak it

43:40 with a few bullets here. And I'm a

43:42 teacher at heart. So, there must be a

43:45 lesson here somewhere.

43:47 So, here goes.

43:48 Lessons I've learned

43:51 from 30 years in the public schools of

43:53 Brookline.

43:54 So, lesson number one is this.

43:57 Follow your heart.

43:58 Don't be afraid to take a chance on

44:01 love.

44:03 Give up your tenured teaching position

44:05 in Connecticut, move away from the

44:07 familiar, move away from your family and

44:09 friends, and follow that special someone

44:13 to an exciting new city.

44:16 It might just work out. Thank you,

44:18 Jason. I love you.

44:21 Um number two,

44:24 when your job interview, Bob, you'll

44:27 know this immediately, kicks off with a

44:29 surprise juggling act by the principal,

44:33 okay?

44:34 Do not fear.

44:36 Just consider the metaphor.

44:40 There is much to juggle

44:42 as an educator. We hold many balls in

44:44 the air,

44:45 and some days really do feel like a

44:47 circus.

44:49 Um and when he offers you that job, you

44:53 say yes. Thank you, Tom Cavanagh.

44:56 I started at Baker,

44:58 [applause]

44:59 spent some time here at Town Hall, and

45:00 I'm happily at Beebe right now. Thank

45:03 you, Margaret.

45:05 Um number three, learn from others.

45:08 Words of wisdom from Alyssa Henry.

45:13 Half of teaching is acting.

45:16 Have some fun and put on a performance

45:19 for your students every single day.

45:22 All right.

45:22 Lesson number four, this is a very

45:25 specific one.

45:27 If you happen to be pregnant

45:30 and go into labor

45:32 at school,

45:33 okay?

45:35 Just call Trisha LaHam.

45:38 [cheering]

45:40 Right?

45:42 Luckily, the baby came late, and I'm

45:45 just going to say Cole, who was supposed

45:47 to be here to support me tonight, uh

45:50 [laughter]

45:51 late again.

45:54 All right. Oh, and the same goes for if

45:56 there's a pandemic, just call Trisha.

46:00 Um number five,

46:02 do what is right, not what is easy.

46:05 Thank you to Vicky Milstein for my daily

46:07 reminder of that.

46:10 Equity and inclusion take continuous

46:14 hard work.

46:16 It is better for everyone

46:20 and it is necessary work.

46:23 Please support programs like Scholars

46:28 and Seed, METCO and materials fee

46:32 and Steps to Success.

46:35 All right?

46:37 And

46:40 lastly,

46:42 while caring for children, care for each

46:45 other.

46:46 And I'll try not to get emotional now.

46:49 Teaching is profoundly relational work.

46:52 Colleagues become friends.

46:56 I look around this room and I see so

46:58 many friends.

47:00 We've shared in weddings and births.

47:03 We've supported each other through

47:04 difficulty and loss.

47:06 And perhaps most importantly, we have

47:09 found laughter and joy in this beautiful

47:13 work of teaching.

47:15 For this, I am grateful. Thank you.

47:19 [applause]

47:25 Thank you, Lisa. Paul Larreau Priestley.

47:36 Hello everybody.

47:38 I'm Paul Larreau Priestley. I run the

47:40 Graphic Arts Copy Center at Brookline

47:42 High School.

47:43 I have been there for 31 years.

47:48 That's a long [snorts] time,

47:50 but I still have life left in me.

47:52 So,

47:54 as I reflected on what I should say this

47:57 evening.

47:58 I thought long and hard about how to use

48:01 this time.

48:04 Should I speak to our fighting the

48:06 systemic racism within our schools

48:09 system and the greater community around

48:12 us?

48:14 Or should I

48:16 speak to the challenge of how we have

48:19 addressed the need

48:21 for racial diversity in our classrooms,

48:24 in our staff, in our administration?

48:28 And then I thought, "Well, what about

48:31 the financial equity?

48:33 Financial equity is also important. But

48:35 then guess what? I said, 'No.'"

48:39 I said, "Love."

48:42 Love.

48:44 Love is transformative.

48:46 Love is restorative.

48:49 Love is [snorts] empowering.

48:52 We pour love into each and every student

48:56 with the power of knowledge.

49:01 You know, we also encourage and

49:03 challenge each other in so many ways

49:06 that gives us all the support that we

49:08 need. And that is a beautiful, beautiful

49:11 thing.

49:12 I can count all of you as my friends.

49:17 And you know,

49:18 in this day and time,

49:20 humanity needs

49:22 nothing greater than the power of love.

49:27 My final words to you all might make

49:30 some of you feel uncomfortable.

49:34 But

49:37 I want each and every single one of you

49:39 to know

49:41 that I truly

49:43 love

49:44 each and every one of you.

49:47 In a world

49:48 where we live,

49:50 where people can spread hate

49:53 and negativity to complete strangers,

49:57 hey, I can spread and give love.

50:01 Remember this, lead with love because

50:05 indeed love will conquer all.

50:11 [applause]

50:17 Thank you, Paul. Susan Lipman.

50:24 [applause]

50:29 Um so, thank you so much for having all

50:32 of us here tonight.

50:34 I'm really proud to be here among this

50:37 group of colleagues that include my

50:39 kids' teachers,

50:41 my kids' classmates' parents, and even

50:44 some people I went to Brookline High

50:46 with.

50:47 Not many people can say that they've

50:49 been in the same job for 31 years, and

50:52 33 years if you count my year as a

50:54 building sub and a learning center para

50:56 before that.

50:57 Even fewer people can say that they

50:59 absolutely love going to work every day.

51:03 Pierce School has been an integral part

51:05 of my life since I was 6 years old.

51:08 I entered the shiny new Pierce School as

51:10 a first grader in 1975.

51:13 I spent my entire elementary career

51:16 there and made some of my closest

51:18 friends during those years.

51:20 My mother later taught Chapter 1 or

51:23 Title 1, they kept kind of changing the

51:25 name of it, um

51:27 at Pierce after she could no longer

51:30 teach for the public schools at St.

51:32 Mary's where she had been for years.

51:34 Even later, my own kids and my stepkids

51:37 attended Pierce.

51:39 So, my journey in Brookline began in

51:41 1993

51:43 when the amazing Barbara Shay hired me

51:46 as a building sub at Lincoln School.

51:49 Barbara was incredible.

51:51 Um

51:52 I was getting my master's degree at the

51:54 time and um

51:56 my first year was at the old Lincoln

51:58 School. And now I've come full circle

52:00 because I'm back there as a Pierce

52:02 School teacher.

52:04 Um

52:05 then well

52:07 the next year we moved to the brand new

52:10 Lincoln School which wasn't called the

52:12 new Lincoln School. It was the old

52:14 school, the new school. But um and I

52:15 moved with them. I was a um

52:18 I worked in the learning center while I

52:19 was getting my master's and um

52:22 the following year I was hired as a

52:24 second grade teacher at Pierce School

52:26 by Chris Field who had also been my

52:29 third grade teacher.

52:30 So that was kind of another full circle

52:32 moment at the time.

52:34 So during the past 31 years I've been

52:38 mentored by and now I get to mentor some

52:41 of the most amazing educators in the

52:44 world.

52:45 When I started at Pierce I worked

52:47 alongside colleagues who had been my

52:49 teachers when I went there.

52:51 They all had such a huge impact on the

52:54 person and the teacher that I am today.

52:57 Marie Lavelle who I miss so greatly

53:00 every day was my work mom. She took me

53:03 under her wing from day one

53:05 and now as someone who's been at Pierce

53:07 for a long time

53:09 I really try and emulate her guidance,

53:12 her patience and her love to all the

53:14 teachers and the children.

53:17 Pierce is more than a job for me. It's

53:19 my community. The place that holds my

53:21 childhood memories and the place where

53:23 my own children thrived.

53:25 This year I have a child in my second

53:27 grade class whose mother was in my first

53:30 class I'm sorry, whose aunt was in my

53:32 first class at Pierce and whose mother

53:35 was in class with my stepson and also a

53:39 Suzanne's too. Um

53:42 Um being a being a teacher, a mom, an

53:46 alumni, and a resident of Brookline

53:48 has really made it to It's I had to

53:51 juggle a lot of hats throughout the

53:52 years. However, I wouldn't change a

53:55 thing, and I love every single minute of

53:58 it. I love walking down the street and

54:00 getting hugs from kids who are 30 and

54:04 kids who are seven.

54:06 I love seeing a Brookline police officer

54:09 and then realizing that that police

54:11 officer was in my second grade class.

54:14 And I still love being an integral part

54:17 of the school that I entered as a first

54:19 grader many, many years ago.

54:22 I want to congratulate everyone who has

54:25 made it for these 30 or 31 years.

54:28 Not every day is easy, especially this

54:30 time of the year. We're all kind of

54:31 crawling to the finish, but um

54:34 but for those of us who have been here

54:36 as long as all of us, we know what a

54:38 special community Brookline is. So,

54:41 thanks so much for having all of us.

54:43 [applause]

54:49 Thank you, Susan. Stephanie Poon.

54:54 [applause]

55:02 Thank you so much for this incredibly

55:04 lovely event. It is a delight to listen

55:07 to you all.

55:09 So, I've been thinking about two things

55:11 uh leading up to tonight. What has

55:13 sustained me for 30 years? Cuz that's a

55:15 long time.

55:16 And what have I noticed uh about what

55:18 has changed and stayed the same since I

55:21 started at BHS

55:23 in the '90s.

55:27 Here's what I came up with.

55:28 The things that have made this job

55:29 possible and even, dare I say,

55:31 occasionally joyful are the people I'm

55:33 surrounded by um um BHS. These are my

55:36 students first and foremost, um who,

55:39 though they might ask me a question

55:41 about something I have literally just

55:42 said, are truly the reason I keep going.

55:45 They are endlessly curious, maddening,

55:48 silly, smart, hard working, and

55:51 constantly make me think about how I can

55:53 do my job better.

55:54 This is the time of year when I really

55:56 lean into the thought that next year, I

55:58 will in fact figure out how to do this

56:00 job and solve all the problems of

56:02 education.

56:05 The other people around me at Brookline

56:06 High that make this possible are my

56:07 colleagues.

56:09 I love them.

56:10 If I didn't have such smart, thoughtful,

56:12 caring people around me, I would go

56:14 bonkers and quit.

56:17 The other thing that's may has made this

56:18 whole 30-year thing possible is all the

56:20 stuff I've been able to do.

56:23 I've mentored new teachers at Brookline

56:25 for years through the Teachers Mentoring

56:26 Teachers program.

56:28 When a group of my veteran colleagues

56:30 had the same conversation for the 15th

56:33 time about how dysfunctional standard

56:35 ninth grade history was, and how

56:37 unsatisfied we were with the arbitrary

56:39 social tracking that led to 70% of our

56:41 students taking honors, we, along with

56:44 the Office of Teaching and Learning, the

56:46 Superintendent, and the School

56:47 Committee,

56:48 created WISP.

56:50 When a different group of colleagues

56:51 lamented the senioritis that afflicts

56:53 our second semester seniors,

56:55 and the ways in which their only

56:57 interest in education seemed to be their

56:59 grades rather than their learning, we

57:01 created an experiential senior class

57:03 called EPIC.

57:05 Giving teachers opportunities to use our

57:07 vast experience and training to invent

57:09 new things and solve problems

57:12 nourishes us.

57:14 I was also sustained because I got to

57:16 watch my own children move through

57:17 Brookline schools, and there is nothing

57:20 so anchoring as seeing your child reap

57:22 the benefits of your colleagues'

57:24 efforts, to feel the love and care

57:26 showered upon them, and know that you

57:28 can never really pay it back, only

57:30 forward.

57:32 The materials fee program that allowed

57:33 my husband and I to send our children,

57:35 one of whom is right back there,

57:38 through the PSB gave me a deeper sense

57:40 of commitment to this place. As my

57:42 husband Brian Lee Brian recently said,

57:44 it literally meant that I had skin in

57:46 the game.

57:47 Do I want to make my school the best it

57:48 can be, to dedicate myself to my

57:50 students the way that my colleagues did

57:52 for my two children?

57:53 You bet you.

57:56 So, the second thing that I've been

57:57 thinking about is what has changed and

57:59 what has stayed the same.

58:01 And really the first two points from

58:03 earlier about the students and the

58:04 colleagues,

58:06 those haven't changed. I mean, they

58:08 literally have changed. They're

58:09 different people. Um, for the most part,

58:12 uh, but the spirit, the energy, the

58:14 inspiration, and the fun, that's pretty

58:16 similar.

58:17 Lots of things have changed, of course.

58:19 When I started, I had binders filled

58:21 with lesson plans, and I wrote on a

58:23 chalkboard.

58:26 I spent a huge amount of time just

58:28 trying to learn all the things I needed

58:29 to teach, acting and teaching.

58:34 The students were themselves,

58:37 but less anxious.

58:40 It felt like teachers

58:42 were generally more respected.

58:46 The things that have sustained me aren't

58:48 complicated or esoteric.

58:50 They are the things that sustain us all.

58:53 Meaningful work with good people,

58:55 new challenges that are engaging,

58:57 fun, laughter, and sometimes joy,

59:01 and summer vacation.

59:06 [applause]

59:11 Thank you, Steph.

59:12 Now, is this Malia or Malia?

59:14 Malia.

59:15 Malia Scott. Thank you.

59:19 [applause]

59:28 So, um at our most recent math

59:31 specialist meeting, we spent time

59:33 translating our careers in Brookline

59:35 into years, days, hours, and minutes,

59:38 because that's exactly what we do for

59:40 fun in the math department.

59:43 So,

59:44 please indulge me.

59:46 So, 32 years ago, when I began my

59:50 master's program and full-year

59:52 internship at the Lincoln School,

59:55 I didn't even know what the materials

59:57 fee program was, nor what a profound

1:00:00 impact it would have on my family's

1:00:01 life.

1:00:03 A week and and a half ago, I got up on

1:00:05 stage and graduated my youngest from

1:00:08 Brookline High.

1:00:10 I feel such gratitude for the amazing

1:00:12 education my children received here in

1:00:14 Brookline.

1:00:15 Though my three children, one of whom is

1:00:18 over there,

1:00:19 um are completely different learners,

1:00:22 they all thrived, were challenged, and

1:00:25 were blessed with amazing teachers that

1:00:27 helped them rise to their potential.

1:00:30 I am so thankful that the materials fee

1:00:32 program made this all possible,

1:00:35 as it not only supported my family, but

1:00:38 also allowed me to dedicate my

1:00:39 professional life to the Brookline

1:00:41 community.

1:00:44 5,856

1:00:46 school days ago,

1:00:48 I could never have imagined I would

1:00:50 serve most of my professional career as

1:00:52 a math specialist, given that books and

1:00:55 reading had always been my lifelong

1:00:57 passion.

1:00:58 However, during my early time in

1:01:00 Brookline, I had the privilege of

1:01:02 partnering with such skilled and gifted

1:01:04 math educators as Rose Christiansen,

1:01:07 Nancy Buel, and Hillary Pastor.

1:01:10 They helped me reshape my own identity

1:01:12 as a mathematician and inspired me to

1:01:15 take advantage of professional

1:01:16 development to continually hone my own

1:01:19 instruction.

1:01:20 Most importantly, they revealed the true

1:01:22 joy of teaching mathematics and

1:01:25 instilled in me the belief that every

1:01:28 student is a capable is a capable math

1:01:31 learner.

1:01:32 My math specialist role has always

1:01:34 centered on collaboration and I'm

1:01:36 incredibly fortunate to work alongside

1:01:38 colleagues who are lifelong learners.

1:01:41 They support, challenge, and inspire me

1:01:43 to continuously grow. It has been a true

1:01:46 privilege to collaborate with such

1:01:48 brilliant educators and to work with

1:01:49 them to find creative ways to bring

1:01:51 mathematics to life.

1:01:53 They push me to try hard things and to

1:01:55 stretch my limits and I am enormously

1:01:57 proud of everything we've accomplished

1:01:59 together over the years.

1:02:02 38,064

1:02:04 school hours ago, I did not yet

1:02:06 recognize the impact of a visionary

1:02:09 supportive school leader.

1:02:11 Today, I'm profoundly grateful to have

1:02:13 worked under such exceptional principals

1:02:16 like Barbara Shay, Andrew Bott, and now

1:02:19 Brian Dinitzio.

1:02:20 They have shown incredible faith in me

1:02:22 and by backing my math initiatives at

1:02:24 Lincoln, they have given me the trust,

1:02:26 space, and time to truly to be truly

1:02:29 innovative, to try new things, and to

1:02:31 grow as a math educator.

1:02:33 And finally, 2,283,840

1:02:39 school minutes ago, I could not have

1:02:41 imagined

1:02:43 that the profound joy I felt when I

1:02:45 first began working with students would

1:02:47 still be at the heart of my practice

1:02:48 today. I love being a teacher.

1:02:52 I thrive on dismantling the I'm bad at

1:02:55 math mindset and get genuine fulfillment

1:02:58 from knowing each learner is an

1:02:59 individual with unique strengths and

1:03:01 challenges. In education, there is never

1:03:04 a one-size-fits-all approach. To meet

1:03:07 the needs of our most vulnerable

1:03:09 students, I must constantly grow,

1:03:11 reflect, and learn.

1:03:13 I live for those hard-fought aha moments

1:03:16 when a complex concept finally clicks

1:03:19 and the sudden surge of confidence when

1:03:21 students begin to recognize themselves

1:03:23 as capable problem solvers.

1:03:26 How lucky am I to still be a teacher in

1:03:29 Brookline 137

1:03:33 30,400

1:03:35 seconds later. Thank you.

1:03:39 [applause]

1:03:46 Thank you, Malia. Um I want to remind

1:03:48 people who are still standing that there

1:03:49 are seats three seats here, a bunch of

1:03:51 seats over there on the on my left. So,

1:03:54 why don't you relax? Sit down.

1:03:56 We're going to be here for a little

1:03:57 longer.

1:03:58 Okay, next up, Yasmin Shariff.

1:04:02 [applause]

1:04:07 All right.

1:04:10 Lisa has to take her glasses put them

1:04:12 on. I have to take mine off cuz that's

1:04:15 what's happening. Um

1:04:17 before I start, I just want to thank the

1:04:18 school committee for this. This is

1:04:19 lovely and thank all of you for being

1:04:22 here.

1:04:22 I want to thank my husband who says to

1:04:24 me often, "I don't know how this all

1:04:27 takes you so long. You've been teaching

1:04:28 for all these years." But as all the

1:04:30 teachers in the room know, no matter how

1:04:32 long you've been teaching, it the time

1:04:35 sort of doesn't get less. It just keeps

1:04:38 on going.

1:04:40 Um and I also just want to thank I have

1:04:43 a bunch of people here whose kids I

1:04:45 taught because of the materials fee

1:04:47 program which we've been hearing about

1:04:48 tonight. That made me part of this

1:04:50 community. My friends are Brookline

1:04:52 parents. My kid has Brookline friends.

1:04:55 My my child went through Baker School

1:04:57 and BHS and

1:04:59 and I taught a lot of your kids. I

1:05:01 taught Nicole's kids. I taught Lisa's

1:05:02 kids. I taught Trisha's kids. I taught

1:05:05 Who else in here is in here? I taught

1:05:06 Susan's kids. I mean, I taught I taught

1:05:09 a I taught

1:05:10 I taught on the school committee as a

1:05:12 child.

1:05:13 So, I I've taught a lot of families here

1:05:16 and it really ties us into the

1:05:17 community. So, just a little plug for

1:05:20 that because it really is a special

1:05:21 program that that keeps us here and that

1:05:23 makes us really devoted to this

1:05:25 community.

1:05:26 So, I just want to tell a story that

1:05:29 sort of starts at the beginning and

1:05:30 ends, I guess, today.

1:05:33 Um

1:05:34 I'm sort of one of those 30 31 I'm kind

1:05:36 of a combo cuz I started teaching

1:05:38 student teaching in 1995

1:05:41 and I got my full-time job in January.

1:05:42 So, I'm like 30 and a half years in, I

1:05:45 guess.

1:05:47 But, I wanted to tell a story that

1:05:48 happened the the very first moments of

1:05:50 my teaching career. So, I started

1:05:52 student teaching in the fifth grade at

1:05:53 Baker School in the fall of 1995.

1:05:57 I loved Baker School, the community, and

1:05:59 everything about teaching. So, when a

1:06:01 teacher in the sixth grade was let go at

1:06:03 Thanksgiving, I applied for the sixth

1:06:05 grade job and hoped for the best. I knew

1:06:07 the sixth grade class was a challenging

1:06:09 one and that my principal, the legendary

1:06:11 Tom Cavanaugh,

1:06:13 was really hoping for an experienced

1:06:15 teacher to take the role.

1:06:18 The interview committee included my

1:06:20 principal, the vice principal, a

1:06:22 teacher, and five parents from the

1:06:24 homeroom class. It was my very first

1:06:26 interview and it was really

1:06:28 intimidating.

1:06:29 Um but, I figured if nothing else, it

1:06:32 was good practice for a future job.

1:06:35 So, I did the interview and I headed

1:06:36 home for winter break and about a week

1:06:38 later, I got a call from Mr. Cavanaugh.

1:06:41 He told me that the parents in the

1:06:42 interview committee were adamant about

1:06:44 hiring me and then said, as only Tom

1:06:47 could, "It's against my better judgment,

1:06:52 but I'm offering you the sixth grade

1:06:54 position."

1:06:57 [laughter]

1:06:57 [gasps]

1:06:58 I was so excited and I promised him I

1:07:00 would not let him down and I would work

1:07:01 really hard to do a good job.

1:07:04 So, Tom's instinct for wanting an

1:07:05 experienced teacher was a good one

1:07:07 because when I started that January, it

1:07:10 was really hard. There was a wide

1:07:12 variety of behavior issues and learning

1:07:14 needs in the sixth grade and in my

1:07:16 homeroom in particular. The students

1:07:18 were also really down on themselves. The

1:07:20 first thing the students in my homeroom

1:07:22 class said to me when I walked in the

1:07:23 room and introduced myself was that I

1:07:26 was going to hate their class.

1:07:28 I know, it was terrible.

1:07:30 They said all the teachers hated their

1:07:32 class and I was going to hate them, too.

1:07:35 I remember looking around the room and

1:07:36 saying to them, "You're my very first

1:07:38 class. I'm going to love you." And we're

1:07:41 going to have a great rest of the year.

1:07:43 But in my mind, I was worried.

1:07:47 I was 23. I looked about 15 and I was

1:07:51 untested in a classroom by myself.

1:07:54 I remember calling home to stop bending

1:07:56 nights that year, worried about all the

1:07:58 classroom behaviors I was struggling

1:07:59 with and wondering if I was getting

1:08:01 through to these kids at all.

1:08:03 My mom, a former teacher, just kept

1:08:05 saying to me, "If you can get through

1:08:07 this year, you can get through

1:08:08 anything."

1:08:09 So, I kept plugging away, letting my

1:08:11 students know how much I cared about

1:08:13 them, how capable they were, and hoping

1:08:15 to create some positivity in the room

1:08:17 from the negativity that had greeted me

1:08:19 at the start.

1:08:20 As we began to close in on the end of

1:08:22 the year, I heard a commotion in the

1:08:23 hallway, which is never good.

1:08:26 I started out of the room to make sure

1:08:27 everything was okay, but what I heard

1:08:29 made me pause. Some of the kids in one

1:08:31 of the other classes were putting down

1:08:33 some of the kids in my class. And the

1:08:35 kids in my class responded by saying,

1:08:36 "Oh, yeah? Well, we're the smart class."

1:08:40 I got a little teary and felt so much

1:08:42 joy inside that we had come from a place

1:08:44 where these students had had such a

1:08:45 negative perception of themselves and

1:08:47 felt like everyone hated them to feeling

1:08:49 like a group of kids who were smart and

1:08:50 worthwhile, which of course they were.

1:08:53 It's always stuck with me, the

1:08:54 importance of kids feeling worthy and

1:08:56 capable and loved in a classroom.

1:08:59 Today, 31 years later, I'm still happily

1:09:01 at Baker in the same position that I

1:09:03 took that December day in 1995.

1:09:06 While all of us in the field of

1:09:08 education are hired to teach academics

1:09:10 and help students learn and grow, one of

1:09:12 the most important lessons of my whole

1:09:14 31-year career happened in that first 6

1:09:17 months of teaching.

1:09:18 If students don't feel seen or cared

1:09:20 about or valued, no matter what I teach

1:09:22 them, it isn't going to stick.

1:09:24 As I move closer to retirement, not that

1:09:26 close, but closer,

1:09:29 and I think back over the close to 2,500

1:09:32 Brookline students I've taught up to

1:09:34 this point, I know they may not remember

1:09:36 what every organelle in a cell does, but

1:09:39 I hope they remember that I cared about

1:09:41 them as a person, I believed in them as

1:09:44 a learner, and that their curiosity,

1:09:46 resilience, and voice matter.

1:09:51 [applause]

1:09:57 Thank you, Esme. Um

1:09:58 Tom Cavana, he was a very smart guy.

1:10:02 Made very good decisions on teachers.

1:10:05 Okay, Francesca Stark.

1:10:09 [applause]

1:10:16 Hi everybody. Um

1:10:18 So, I'm Francesca Stark, and I um

1:10:22 wanted to say I everything everybody

1:10:25 already said, so that's it.

1:10:28 Pretty much though. Um I was thinking

1:10:31 about why I came to Brookline, why I

1:10:34 stayed in Brookline, and what I hope for

1:10:37 Brookline.

1:10:38 Um I came to Brookline because um my mom

1:10:42 was a struggling bohemian painter, and

1:10:44 she couldn't afford my braces, and so um

1:10:47 she decided to become a teacher, and she

1:10:49 heard about the Brookline schools, which

1:10:51 we

1:10:52 have heard that story many time about

1:10:53 hearing about the Brookline schools. So,

1:10:54 we moved to Westbourne Terrace to a

1:10:56 rent-controlled apartment, and I went to

1:10:58 Driscoll School.

1:11:00 Um there was a group uh they had uh

1:11:03 guidance counselors for everything then,

1:11:05 including uh uh children of single moms.

1:11:08 And uh there was a guidance counselor,

1:11:11 and she had these little rubber dolls uh

1:11:13 where you would act out probably some

1:11:15 kind of trauma that they I don't know

1:11:17 what they were for, but we would act

1:11:18 them out. And later when I got hired in

1:11:20 Brookline, I was like, "Oh, I'm one of

1:11:22 the little rubber dolls now." Like there

1:11:24 was the the firefighter rubber doll and

1:11:26 the nurse rubber doll, and I went to

1:11:28 school. So, I um So, what happened is I

1:11:32 um

1:11:34 I had a very good experience at

1:11:35 Driscoll. I had a very bad fourth-grade

1:11:37 teacher uh who um

1:11:39 would mock kids in the class. And um

1:11:43 And I always wanted to be a teacher, not

1:11:45 really because of her, but I wanted to

1:11:46 be a teacher that wasn't like her. So,

1:11:49 um what happened is uh

1:11:52 my mom told me, "Don't ever become a

1:11:54 teacher." Because after she was a

1:11:55 bohemian artist, uh she became a

1:11:57 teacher. And she said, "They will not

1:12:00 trust your intellect. They will not

1:12:01 allow you to do art. You will never be

1:12:04 free to be who you are. You will have to

1:12:06 follow a a structured curriculum. Don't

1:12:10 do it." And I immediately decided to do

1:12:12 it. And um so, I became a teacher, and

1:12:15 uh no, wait, stop. I was wasn't sure

1:12:19 what to do, and I was walking down the

1:12:20 Fenway, and I was walking by Wheelock

1:12:22 College, and I saw a sign that said,

1:12:25 "Come join the Learning Teaching

1:12:27 Collaborative, where teachers can be

1:12:28 leaders." And I was like, "My mom would

1:12:30 like that." So, that's how I ended up.

1:12:32 So, I um

1:12:34 I walked in, and this woman named Joan

1:12:36 Gatto said, um um "Did you hear about

1:12:39 the job opening, the intern opening?"

1:12:41 And I said, "No." So, anyway, it was to

1:12:44 be an intern for somebody named Vivian

1:12:46 Troen um at the uh formerly known uh

1:12:49 Devotion School, and I was fortunate to

1:12:52 be her intern. So, I became a full year

1:12:55 intern in Vivian's class, and I was the

1:12:58 luckiest person alive because everybody

1:13:00 was intellectual, and they were

1:13:02 interested in teaching and collaboration

1:13:04 and and studying kids and and learning

1:13:07 about kids and spending time at morning

1:13:09 meeting so that you could learn about

1:13:10 kids, and so I thought this is it. And

1:13:13 then after that, Carol Schraft hired me

1:13:16 because she found out I had been a

1:13:18 student in the teacher's class. She knew

1:13:21 about that teacher and knew that that

1:13:23 teacher wasn't a very good teacher, and

1:13:25 she was like, "Well, if you want to now

1:13:26 teach after being in that teacher's

1:13:28 class, you're hired." So, I got hired.

1:13:30 Um

1:13:31 Anyway, all of that is to say that I um

1:13:36 really loved being an intern for the

1:13:38 full year, and then I ended up getting

1:13:40 hired because I really knew Brookline. I

1:13:43 mean, I really knew Brookline. But, I um

1:13:46 was part of the learning teaching

1:13:47 collaborative, which Betsy Fitzpatrick

1:13:50 uh used to give us vouchers to um Well,

1:13:53 you didn't pay for them, but you handed

1:13:54 them out. And uh

1:13:56 and uh

1:13:57 and we could take courses, and we could

1:13:59 do a lot of very interesting things. And

1:14:02 um so, anyway, what kept me at Brookline

1:14:05 was all the things you all said. My My

1:14:07 husband, who's over there, he didn't go

1:14:09 to the Brookline schools, but our kids

1:14:10 went to the Brookline schools, and um

1:14:14 and we love it here, and I live on

1:14:15 Westbourne Terrace now. I know it's a

1:14:17 little sad, but I do. I mean, it's not

1:14:19 that sad. But, I'm definitely a local.

1:14:21 So, um but why I bring this up is now

1:14:23 about what I want for Brookline.

1:14:26 Um so, here's the thing. I totally agree

1:14:28 with Liz said. Teaching never gets

1:14:30 easier, and everybody kept saying, "Does

1:14:32 it get easier?" I kept saying, "No, it

1:14:33 really doesn't get any easier." The

1:14:35 thing that's getting harder now

1:14:38 is that some of the things my mom said

1:14:40 are true are coming a little bit true.

1:14:43 And that's what I want to talk to the

1:14:45 school committee about and and is that

1:14:48 more and more the curriculum feels very

1:14:51 um like a conveyor belt of curriculum.

1:14:53 This is my personal opinion. I'm a fifth

1:14:55 grade teacher. It's not true for

1:14:56 everybody, I know. But,

1:14:59 there's not enough time to get to know

1:15:01 the kids. There's There's not enough

1:15:03 time for morning meeting. I do it every

1:15:05 single day and the kids love it, but we

1:15:08 have to push so many things else aside.

1:15:11 We need to have more So, I'm telling you

1:15:13 now what I think. Sorry, but we need to

1:15:15 have more vertical alignment, so all the

1:15:18 teachers know and there's time to see

1:15:20 what everybody's done. We need to have

1:15:23 synthesize all our curriculum because

1:15:25 there's just not enough time to teach

1:15:26 everything. And um we need to have more

1:15:29 teacher input and time for teacher

1:15:31 collaboration and I really think we need

1:15:33 to bring the mentor the internship

1:15:35 program back because having a second

1:15:37 teacher in your classroom all year

1:15:40 gives you time to do things like go pee,

1:15:42 which um I have a a urologist just

1:15:45 parent in my classroom and she's like,

1:15:46 "Teachers are the worst." I'm like, "I

1:15:48 know." We really are. So, we need to

1:15:51 have people who are working together all

1:15:53 year. Bring those teachers into our

1:15:56 school system. I brought the 1996

1:15:59 version of how to do it. I had to dig it

1:16:00 up. I really did. So, um that's really

1:16:04 what I want to say. I I I love the I

1:16:06 love teaching. I I I love my colleagues

1:16:09 and I I think now it's it's time to get

1:16:12 back to saying what we value and doing

1:16:16 what we value. Thank you so much.

1:16:19 [applause]

1:16:26 Thank you, Francesca.

1:16:29 Allison Whitebone.

1:16:33 [applause]

1:16:39 Hi, I'm Allison and I teach ninth grade

1:16:42 at Brookline High School. Thank you so

1:16:44 much for honoring us tonight. 31 years

1:16:46 goes by like

1:16:48 the snap of a fingers. So crazy. Thanks

1:16:51 to my mom for being here

1:16:54 and my husband and

1:16:55 [applause]

1:16:56 son and daughter. Hi, say hi. Okay.

1:16:59 Um

1:17:00 I love teaching. I love Brookline High

1:17:02 School and I really wasn't sure what to

1:17:04 talk about today, but one of my students

1:17:08 in his journal um was writing about

1:17:10 Holden Caulfield and how he hates change

1:17:14 and my student said he hated change, but

1:17:16 through writing in his journal all year

1:17:19 and accomplishing 236 handwritten pages,

1:17:23 he decided he accepts change and he

1:17:26 loves it. So, I thought, I'm going to

1:17:28 start with that about change and it

1:17:30 reminded me

1:17:31 of where I started, opportunity for

1:17:33 change.

1:17:35 And that's what Brookline means to me

1:17:38 and Bob, you're a part of starting that

1:17:40 before I got there, but Brookline

1:17:43 created a wonderful program for who

1:17:46 students who needed it.

1:17:48 They created a family of teachers with

1:17:50 Bill Grady and Bill Hibbert and Jack

1:17:53 Liss and

1:17:54 um Eileen Lombardi and all these other

1:17:57 great people and Bob Gray worked with us

1:17:59 from the town

1:18:01 and it created this wonderful feeling of

1:18:05 family, um consistency, clarity and

1:18:08 relationship

1:18:10 that has really

1:18:12 I've just continued that for all of

1:18:14 these 31 years and it just has it's a

1:18:17 calling. The poetry festival with Lynn

1:18:20 Cohen and all of the librarians

1:18:23 for 31 years now thanks to the PTO. It's

1:18:26 all collaboration. It's all family and

1:18:28 everyone telling their stories and

1:18:30 Geraldine Mondy and her family of all

1:18:33 the siblings telling their stories and

1:18:36 my English Department friends, and Ed

1:18:39 Weiser, who I went to graduate school

1:18:41 with. Remember our project on

1:18:43 technology? And now I started off by

1:18:45 talking about handwriting in the

1:18:47 journal.

1:18:48 And so, um

1:18:50 [laughter]

1:18:51 it's the best technology there is. But,

1:18:54 um

1:18:55 long story short, I just want to say

1:18:57 Brookline it public schools, it's a

1:18:59 wonderful family. We take care of our

1:19:02 children. We take care of our staff. And

1:19:06 I love the collaboration, and I'm

1:19:08 forever grateful for these years. And

1:19:10 I'm not done yet. I'm not done. I'm

1:19:12 still young. My mom's 86. Sorry, Mom.

1:19:15 And she still works. So, all right.

1:19:17 [laughter]

1:19:18 I love you. Okay. Thank you so much to

1:19:20 my whole team, and to everyone here

1:19:22 today. Thank you.

1:19:24 [applause]

1:19:30 Thank you, Ally.

1:19:32 Ed Weiser, you're it.

1:19:37 Thank you. Thank you.

1:19:38 [applause]

1:19:40 Um yeah, good evening. Thanks for

1:19:41 celebrating with all of us. Thank you

1:19:43 for staying for the the last person to

1:19:45 go.

1:19:46 Um and um so, this is going to be

1:19:49 gratitude to the Brookline community,

1:19:50 but also to the BEF in particular. Um if

1:19:53 I could go back three decades and tell

1:19:55 myself that my teaching career would

1:19:57 involve skydiving,

1:19:59 flying helicopters, and eventually

1:20:00 becoming an EMT, yes, uh driving an

1:20:03 ambulance on the weekends, um to teach

1:20:05 students about the medical profession, I

1:20:07 never would have believed it.

1:20:08 Um I I've had the opportunity to have so

1:20:10 many adventures. Those I mentioned um

1:20:13 were all supported by the BEF.

1:20:16 Um but, for one wild opportunity, and

1:20:18 Bob, I don't remember where the money

1:20:19 came from this. You might have pulled

1:20:20 some strings, but I went to a conference

1:20:22 at the Fermi Lab, a super collider, in

1:20:24 Illinois for um the first summer,

1:20:26 and I met Nobel laureate Leon Lederman,

1:20:28 and eventually launched our physics

1:20:30 first program for ninth graders.

1:20:32 BHS became the leader in this simple way

1:20:35 to gain much needed equity in who took

1:20:37 physics and all of the science courses

1:20:40 beyond.

1:20:41 So much so that Jim Walsh,

1:20:43 right there, um

1:20:45 even shocked uh DESI

1:20:47 because um they started to create this

1:20:49 MCAS exam for science and he said,

1:20:52 "You better include physics." And

1:20:53 people's jaws dropped.

1:20:55 So um and also when Professor Lederman

1:20:57 would get a press inquiry, he would he

1:20:59 was too lazy so he would just send it on

1:21:00 to me. So that was quite a stunning

1:21:02 privilege. I was interviewed by the New

1:21:04 York Times, the Journal, all that kind

1:21:06 of stuff.

1:21:07 Then after um

1:21:08 11 years, Bob trusted me to lead the

1:21:11 science department. And we grew uh

1:21:13 opportunities for students.

1:21:15 And we grew the physical campus of BHS

1:21:17 to meet the future. And um but a true

1:21:20 point of pride is uh being the leader of

1:21:22 the team that was con- consistently

1:21:24 ranked in the top STEM schools in the

1:21:26 nation.

1:21:28 And um and our classrooms right now are

1:21:29 thriving with creativity, inclusion, and

1:21:31 rigor.

1:21:32 I'm really I'm proud of that. Um but you

1:21:35 should know that during the building

1:21:36 project, I advocated for a generous

1:21:38 equipment budget, which included a

1:21:40 medical simulation lab. You can see the

1:21:42 three mannequins on the the third floor

1:21:44 of the STEM wing. Um they might creep

1:21:47 you out a little bit, but

1:21:48 they're good.

1:21:49 Sorry, I know it creeps you out unless

1:21:51 all the time. Um but one thing led to

1:21:53 another and um the the BEF came back and

1:21:56 stepped in and helped me become uh an

1:21:58 EMT. So now you can find me um teaching

1:22:02 students how to make a differential

1:22:03 diagnosis for Hal, not Mason, Hal the

1:22:06 mannequin, that's his name,

1:22:09 when uh

1:22:10 when he presents to our ED for a

1:22:12 shortness of breath and altered mental

1:22:13 status.

1:22:14 Um so that's at the point where I

1:22:16 stepped away from the leadership role to

1:22:17 expand the medical careers offerings uh

1:22:20 to continue to pretend to be a doctor,

1:22:23 um slipping in robotics, woodworking,

1:22:24 and the new construction class as well.

1:22:26 Something I never could have imagined

1:22:28 when Bob flicked on the light. Do you

1:22:30 remember that during my interview? That

1:22:32 was your classic. You'd go over and

1:22:34 flick the lights on and off and ask all

1:22:35 of the physics candidates if they knew

1:22:37 what was going on. Just to see if they

1:22:38 could explain the physics of AC circuit.

1:22:41 Almost almost nobody knew. It was

1:22:42 bizarre.

1:22:43 It was I knew.

1:22:44 [laughter]

1:46:03 Well, um

1:46:05 it's I guess uh surreal for me to be at

1:46:08 the end of this uh journey in my life

1:46:12 um and to be here at this moment to

1:46:14 share it with you.

1:46:16 These last 3 years have without a doubt

1:46:18 been challenging, and I've had to bring

1:46:20 to bear every

1:46:22 single learning experience of my life

1:46:24 [laughter]

1:46:25 um to kind of navigate through um each

1:46:29 and every

1:46:30 uh challenge that has uh arisen.

1:46:33 But, I have to say uh that is what um

1:46:36 I guess I was raised for, right? I mean,

1:46:38 you come to moments in your life

1:46:41 um and you don't really appreciate

1:46:44 um what you've learned from every

1:46:46 experience that you've had until you

1:46:47 face yet another experience where it

1:46:51 requires

1:46:52 um

1:46:53 every ounce of fortitude that you have

1:46:55 in order to get through.

1:46:57 And I think one of the blessings of uh

1:46:59 being

1:47:01 um along in your career

1:47:03 is that you get a pretty

1:47:05 uh centeredness about yourself.

1:47:08 You know certain things to be true,

1:47:10 and it helps you to navigate through

1:47:12 difficult times.

1:47:14 You know, you don't You don't concern

1:47:17 yourself with public opinion,

1:47:20 um and you're able to move forward

1:47:23 grounded in your values, understandings,

1:47:25 and beliefs.

1:47:26 I know that, uh, what motivates me each

1:47:29 and every day

1:47:31 is doing something that helps

1:47:34 improve education for students.

1:47:38 That is what I am about.

1:47:40 It's my hope that the work that I've

1:47:42 done here will allow you in the future

1:47:45 to be able to focus on the instructional

1:47:47 minute and not on administration all the

1:47:52 So, if I've done one small thing to get

1:47:59 for allowing me this opportunity to

1:50:19 Betsy, I think we have a bunch, right?
