1:50:35 One moment.

1:50:36 You can hear you guys now.

1:50:38 Okay.

1:50:44 Hey, sorry did you call on me? I wasn't

1:50:45 sure. I was just turned into a panelist.

1:50:48 Go early.

1:50:49 Thanks, hi. Um my name's Arly Butler. Um

1:50:53 I'm a Brooklyn resident and FR parent to

1:50:54 three children, a room parent, a PTO

1:50:56 volunteer. And next year, my youngest

1:50:57 son, Ben, will transition from BEEP to

1:50:59 kindergarten at FR.

1:51:01 I'm here tonight, or at least virtually,

1:51:03 um to respectfully urge the district to

1:51:05 reconsider the decision to reduce FR's

1:51:07 kindergarten from five sections down to

1:51:09 four, or at the very least to pause this

1:51:11 decision until fall enrollment numbers

1:51:12 are fully finalized.

1:51:15 [cough]

1:51:15 Excuse me. Kindergarten [clears throat]

1:51:16 is a profound developmental transition.

1:51:18 For five-year-olds coming from small

1:51:19 preschools, entering FRR, already

1:51:21 largest elementary school in Brookline,

1:51:22 is an enormous adjustment. Class size

1:51:25 matters intensely at this age. Larger

1:51:27 classes mean less individual attention

1:51:28 during a critical stage of growth. Fewer

1:51:30 eyes on the children who may be quietly

1:51:31 struggling, and less support as they

1:51:33 learn how to navigate a large public

1:51:34 school.

1:51:35 The district's current rationale is that

1:51:37 overall overall numbers are low, placing

1:51:39 FRR at an average of 19.5 students per

1:51:41 class. But looking at this data from an

1:51:43 equity lens tells a different story. If

1:51:44 you cross-analyze ELL and IEP data

1:51:46 across the district, FRR carries the

1:51:48 highest numbers by far. We have more

1:51:49 students with complex additional needs,

1:51:51 but now fewer teachers to support them.

1:51:54 Furthermore, framing the removal of the

1:51:55 K section as a budgetary balance to fund

1:51:57 necessary fifth grade section

1:51:59 doesn't make sense. The needs of a fifth

1:52:02 grader and a kindergartner are entirely

1:52:03 separate. Our youngest learners learners

1:52:05 should not have to compromise their

1:52:07 foundational year. If the district must

1:52:09 cut a section for budgetary

1:52:10 self-balance, it makes no sense under an

1:52:13 equity lens to target FRR. Within

1:52:15 Brookline, FRR does not have the

1:52:17 smallest K classes. It's unjust to pull

1:52:19 a section from a school that already has

1:52:20 a higher baseline enrollment and a crowd

1:52:22 of our and crowd our students, while

1:52:24 leaving schools with significant

1:52:26 lower enrollment intact.

1:52:28 Finally, relying strictly on June 1st

1:52:30 numbers is premature. We know enrollment

1:52:31 fluctuates fluctuates over the summer.

1:52:33 In fact, from simply talking to my

1:52:35 community, I know of five families with

1:52:37 rising kindergartners who are currently

1:52:38 moving to the neighborhood. They fully

1:52:40 plan to attend FRR this fall, but due to

1:52:42 some removing logistics, they have not

1:52:43 yet been able to officially register.

1:52:45 Once a section is cut, it is incredibly

1:52:46 difficult to reverse. Many of us in the

1:52:48 community stepped up to support the

1:52:49 override because we believed it would

1:52:50 protect protect the foundational quality

1:52:52 of Brookline education. Crowding our

1:52:54 incoming kindergarten classes is not the

1:52:55 quality of experience we voted to

1:52:57 preserve. I urge you to keep the fifth

1:52:59 FRR kindergarten section open until fall

1:53:00 enrollment is clear, and to communicate

1:53:02 transparently with our incoming

1:53:03 families. Thank you for your time, for

1:53:05 your hard work, and all your

1:53:06 consideration.

1:53:07 Thank you, Willie.

1:53:11 Is Midor- Midori here?

1:53:14 Midori Nagano?

1:53:26 [clears throat]

1:53:28 Uh, two here.

1:53:30 Yeah.

1:53:31 Um, uh, greeting. Uh, my name is

1:53:33 Masahiro Nagano and I'm a parent of the

1:53:36 two children in F.A. Rawson community.

1:53:38 Um, my old son, uh, Sora is currently in

1:53:40 grade one and my younger son, Haru, is

1:53:42 in pre-K. Um, our home is in the buffer

1:53:45 zone between the Pierce and Lawrence,

1:53:47 but F.A. Rawson is the closest school to

1:53:49 our home.

1:53:50 So, when we moved to the Brookline 2

1:53:52 years ago, uh, we requested to F.A.

1:53:54 Rawson and were, uh, accepted without

1:53:56 any difficulty. Uh, since then, our

1:53:59 school have been, uh, part of F.A.

1:54:00 Rawson community for 2 years and during

1:54:03 that time, like, we have never moved and

1:54:05 last year we received, uh, no school

1:54:07 reassignment at all. Um, we naturally

1:54:10 expected that, like the other part of

1:54:12 the community, uh, we would spend the

1:54:13 coming year at F.A. Rawson. However, uh,

1:54:16 the for the next year, uh, the both of

1:54:18 our children, uh,

1:54:20 including my older son, uh, were

1:54:22 suddenly assigned to the Pierce School.

1:54:24 Uh, we understand that, like, school

1:54:25 assignment is a complicated matter, uh,

1:54:28 but I want to ask one simple question,

1:54:30 like, do you really believe this is a

1:54:31 good thing of our children, uh, to force

1:54:35 uh, current and old student to leave our

1:54:37 school community, uh, where they have

1:54:39 already spent years growing, adjusting

1:54:42 and building our a sense of belonging?

1:54:44 Uh, when Sora first entered, uh, F.A.

1:54:47 Rawson, he struggled and to adjust a new

1:54:49 environment and even

1:54:51 needed support from our school

1:54:53 counselor. Uh, only recently has he, uh,

1:54:55 built a stable and confident, uh,

1:54:58 deeply connected to the school. Uh, both

1:55:00 of our son, uh, love F.A. Rawson and

1:55:02 they know the teachers and buildings and

1:55:05 the children, and

1:55:07 every day after school they happily

1:55:09 operate with their friends at

1:55:11 playgrounds.

1:55:12 So,

1:55:13 when they heard they might be forced to

1:55:15 transfer the proposed our

1:55:18 both of my sons

1:55:20 cried and asked why they had to be

1:55:22 separated from their friends.

1:55:25 So, that until last year I knew multiple

1:55:27 families in which the younger siblings

1:55:29 are just follow to the older siblings at

1:55:32 the same school regardless of the

1:55:34 district.

1:55:36 So,

1:55:38 the many F R R parents have told us that

1:55:41 they are deeply confused with the

1:55:43 decision that happened to us.

1:55:46 We saw the same reaction as a coffee

1:55:47 conversation where the many of the PTOs

1:55:50 and the school principal

1:55:52 attended.

1:55:53 So, this is not just about our family.

1:55:56 I'm also here to express the concern

1:55:57 about the plan to eliminate one

1:56:00 kindergarten class at F R R. So, if one

1:56:02 kindergarten class is count like average

1:56:04 school class size will rise

1:56:06 significantly,

1:56:08 which is not fair in terms of the

1:56:09 educational equity.

1:56:11 So, we feel that like

1:56:13 our unfair the school assignment was

1:56:16 caused by

1:56:17 in a sense in order to reduce simply the

1:56:20 number of the students as much as

1:56:22 possible.

1:56:23 So, all together I respectfully ask the

1:56:26 district and school community to

1:56:27 reconsider both the kindergarten class

1:56:30 reduction at F R R and also like the

1:56:32 decision that happened to us

1:56:34 forcing already enrolled the students

1:56:38 like our son to transfer. So, these are

1:56:40 not just a number on the spreadsheet.

1:56:43 These are the real children, real

1:56:44 families, and a real school community.

1:56:47 Thank you so much.

1:56:51 Thank you, Midori.

1:56:53 Next up is Jordana Spitz. Jordana?

1:56:57 By Zoom?

1:57:01 Hello.

1:57:06 My name's Jordana

1:57:07 guidance counselor at Ridley School for

1:57:08 the K-2 kiddos. I've written like 20

1:57:11 speeches in the past couple weeks cuz I

1:57:12 feel really passionately about the cut

1:57:15 at Ridley School. And so I just want to

1:57:16 give you some numbers about enrollment

1:57:18 because I'm the one who meets with all

1:57:20 the families when they come in over the

1:57:22 summer and during the school year as

1:57:24 well. So, um our numbers are the highest

1:57:29 that they have ever been because of the

1:57:30 cut of sections. And so I want to just

1:57:33 share some data about um the enrollment

1:57:36 and the enrollment specifically around

1:57:38 Hebrew-speaking families that we cannot

1:57:40 buffer out. So, this past school year

1:57:42 during July 1st to the end of the school

1:57:46 year, Ridley enrolled 109 students

1:57:49 including 82 who registered over the

1:57:51 summer.

1:57:53 Of those, 14 were kindergarteners,

1:57:56 11 whom were Hebrew speakers.

1:57:59 The year before that, 2024-2025, Ridley

1:58:01 enrolled 118 students, 78 over the

1:58:05 summer. Of those students, again, 14

1:58:08 were kindergarteners, 10 of whom were

1:58:10 Hebrew speakers, and additional 40

1:58:13 students enrolled over the school year

1:58:16 including nine more kindergarteners.

1:58:18 Um of those, those children who are here

1:58:21 in Brookline reside all over Brookline

1:58:23 but come to Ridley because of the

1:58:26 Hebrew-speaking program and they cannot

1:58:28 be buffered out.

1:58:29 Um Ridley is also, as you probably have

1:58:32 heard so many times, a uniquely complex

1:58:34 community, a Title 1 school. We have the

1:58:36 highest population of EL learners. We

1:58:39 have the highest population of

1:58:40 low-income learners. Sorry, that's my

1:58:42 doggie crying.

1:58:43 TLC program, we serve a

1:58:46 um a homeless shelter in our

1:58:47 neighborhood, and we have the largest

1:58:49 population of families arriving from

1:58:51 Israel every year, many directly

1:58:54 impacted by war and trauma. According to

1:58:57 DESE, 46% of our population qualifies as

1:59:00 high needs. 46, and that's in a school

1:59:03 of almost 900 kids.

1:59:06 I could speak about equity, I could

1:59:07 speak all of that, but I want to speak

1:59:09 about what it feels like on the ground

1:59:10 at Ridley School. Turnover is high. This

1:59:13 year alone, we had three new first grade

1:59:15 teachers and a whole new K-2 learning

1:59:19 center team. Staff often transfer to

1:59:22 other Brookline schools, I've seen it

1:59:24 all the time, and they report back like,

1:59:25 "Wow, it's so different. So different."

1:59:29 Right now, our kindergarten teachers and

1:59:30 I myself, after working here for 20

1:59:33 years, feel deeply discouraged. The

1:59:36 inequity is taking a serious toll. Based

1:59:39 on consistent numbers, we know we will

1:59:41 enroll over 100 students over the

1:59:43 summer, many who are kindergarteners

1:59:46 that cannot be buffered out into other

1:59:47 schools. I respectfully ask that you

1:59:50 reinstate the eliminated section as soon

1:59:53 as possible.

1:59:54 Doing so now would allow us to retain an

1:59:56 outstanding teacher, create a really

2:00:00 balanced classrooms that we work so hard

2:00:02 to do, and a thoughtful school-wide

2:00:04 schedule. We can't do any of these

2:00:05 without knowing our sections. If

2:00:08 decision is delayed until August, we'll

2:00:10 be forced to scramble, have an

2:00:11 inexperienced teacher, reorganize class

2:00:13 placements, and notify more than 80

2:00:16 families of class placement change.

2:00:19 Um, lastly, kindergarten is the place to

2:00:21 save a little bit of money. These kids

2:00:23 are so young. Some of them are learning

2:00:25 to use the bathroom. They're crying.

2:00:27 They're running out of the classroom.

2:00:28 They need a lot of teacher support. No

2:00:31 curriculum is going to make a difference

2:00:33 in that. Um, and I lastly want to thank

2:00:35 Bella for being so open about conversing

2:00:38 with me and having dialogue around this.

2:00:41 I really appreciate that, and I

2:00:42 appreciate your attention to the numbers

2:00:44 over for next few weeks because they

2:00:46 change significantly over the Ridley

2:00:48 school.

2:00:49 Thank you.

2:00:52 [applause]

2:00:53 Thank you very much, Jordana.

2:00:55 Next up, Benny Weingarten-Gabbay.

2:01:04 Not here?

2:01:06 Uh Jessica Jessica Schneider.

2:01:13 [clears throat]

2:01:20 Hi everyone. My name is Jessica

2:01:21 Schneider, and I am a parent at Florida

2:01:24 Ruffin Ridley.

2:01:26 As we meet on the eve of Juneteenth, I

2:01:29 hope we can reflect on how Juneteenth's

2:01:32 values of equality, remembrance, and

2:01:34 historical truth

2:01:35 are expressed in the decisions we make

2:01:37 for students here in Brookline.

2:01:39 Because many families are struggling

2:01:41 with the simple question, "Why is FRR

2:01:45 set to lose a kindergarten?"

2:01:48 Other Brookline K classrooms, including

2:01:50 Lawrence, Lincoln, and Runkle, are

2:01:51 projected to have about 12 to 13

2:01:54 students per class next year, while FRR

2:01:57 is projected to have somewhere between

2:01:59 18 to 20 after losing a class.

2:02:03 Now, I'm not arguing

2:02:05 school every school must have identical

2:02:06 class sizes. I'm asking why the

2:02:09 administration's preferred outcome is

2:02:11 for FRR to have K classes roughly 50%

2:02:14 larger than some other Brookline

2:02:16 schools.

2:02:18 I've received many communications, and

2:02:20 the administration has said factors

2:02:22 other factors were considered, including

2:02:24 staffing, facilities, and enrollment

2:02:26 trends. And I certainly appreciate that

2:02:28 these decisions are complex.

2:02:30 But families are already experiencing

2:02:32 the consequences, like you just heard.

2:02:34 Some are facing the challenge of

2:02:35 siblings attending other schools, and

2:02:37 for many families, especially immigrant

2:02:39 families, changing schools can mean

2:02:41 losing support networks that took years

2:02:43 to build.

2:02:44 What's confusing is that based on the

2:02:46 district's projections, FRR was not the

2:02:49 only school capable of absorbing a

2:02:51 reduction.

2:02:53 So, which leaves me with the question of

2:02:55 which specific factors led the

2:02:57 administration to choose FRR rather than

2:03:00 any of the alternatives.

2:03:02 My next question concerns equity. FRR,

2:03:05 as you know, is a Title 1 school.

2:03:07 Research consistently shows that smaller

2:03:10 class sizes in the early grades improve

2:03:12 outcomes, particularly for historically

2:03:14 underserved students.

2:03:16 Brookline's equity policy commits the

2:03:19 district to identifying and addressing

2:03:21 disparities in educational experiences

2:03:23 and opportunities.

2:03:25 Can this group explain how creating the

2:03:27 largest projected kindergarten classes

2:03:30 in a district at a Title 1 school is

2:03:32 consistent with that commitment?

2:03:35 My final question concerns risk. We've

2:03:37 been told enrollment remains fluid and

2:03:40 that it's too early to know what final

2:03:41 enrollment will look like. But if

2:03:43 enrollment projections are uncertain,

2:03:45 why are they sufficiently certain to

2:03:46 justify eliminating a K class at FRR

2:03:49 today?

2:03:50 And if those projections prove wrong,

2:03:52 FRR begins from a position of having the

2:03:54 largest projected K classes in the

2:03:56 district and may be forced to hire late

2:03:59 in the summer from a smaller teacher

2:04:00 candidate pool.

2:04:02 Since teacher quality matters alongside

2:04:04 class size, a late summer hiring

2:04:06 compounds the educational risks for

2:04:08 students.

2:04:10 What analysis supports concentrating

2:04:12 both the largest projected class sizes,

2:04:14 the greatest enrollment risk, and

2:04:16 teacher quality at Florida Ruffin

2:04:18 Ridley?

2:04:19 I believe everyone involved has the best

2:04:21 of intentions while navigating difficult

2:04:23 trade-offs. I'm asking for transparency

2:04:26 about a decision that appears to place a

2:04:28 disproportionate burden on one school

2:04:30 community. Thank you.

2:04:33 [applause]

2:04:36 Thank you, Jessica. So, Benny wasn't

2:04:38 here. How about Rosen Valtchev? Val-

2:04:41 Valtchev.

2:04:42 Uh they're later. So, doing

2:04:44 [clears throat]

2:04:44 two sections of

2:04:45 Oh, they'll do later?

2:04:45 They were told to come

2:04:46 Oh, they were told. Okay, cool. Okay,

2:04:48 great.

2:04:50 Um item number four on the agenda,

2:04:51 welcome

2:04:52 Annika Banerji from Brookline High

2:04:55 School, our new student representative.

2:04:57 And welcome, welcome, welcome, Annika.

2:05:00 And I've asked Annika to say a few words

2:05:02 about

2:05:03 who she is, what she's doing at the high

2:05:05 school, how she got chosen for this

2:05:07 position.

2:05:08 And yeah, that's it.

2:05:11 Thank you. My name is Annika Banerji and

2:05:13 I'm a sophomore at Brookline High

2:05:15 School. I'm really grateful to be here

2:05:17 in front of all of you and I'm really

2:05:18 looking forward to being the School

2:05:19 Committee representative for the

2:05:20 upcoming 2026 and 2027 school year. Just

2:05:24 a couple things about myself. I grew up

2:05:27 in Singapore and New York City, um both

2:05:29 very large cities with a lot of people.

2:05:32 And when I was in middle school, I moved

2:05:34 here to Brookline. And when I moved here

2:05:36 is when I realized that it's a lot more

2:05:39 than small town charm. I mean, firstly,

2:05:40 we're not a very small town.

2:05:42 And secondly, it's really the unique

2:05:46 dynamics and relationships in this town.

2:05:48 And since I've joined the high school,

2:05:50 I've become a part of our student-run

2:05:52 school newspaper, The Cypress. And there

2:05:55 I've had a chance to tell stories and

2:05:57 listen to stories of many other members

2:05:59 of our community.

2:06:00 And when running for this role, I really

2:06:02 wanted to share the sense of Brookline

2:06:05 that I have with everyone around me. And

2:06:08 I wanted to make sure that Brookline is

2:06:10 not just the town that we live in, but

2:06:13 it's our town that we live in. And I

2:06:15 hope that as a voice for our student

2:06:17 body at Brookline High School, I'm able

2:06:19 to bring some of that.

2:06:21 Thank you.

2:06:24 [applause]

2:06:25 How did How did you get selected for the

2:06:27 position?

2:06:28 Cuz it's pays a lot.

2:06:30 [laughter]

2:06:31 Um so, the requirement was you submitted

2:06:34 a written application and then you came

2:06:37 in for an interview in front of our

2:06:38 student government. And Brookline's very

2:06:40 unique. We have a very complex student

2:06:42 government with multiple branches and

2:06:45 you were required to give a speech to

2:06:47 the student council and then your own

2:06:50 student government would vote on whether

2:06:52 um, you would be an

2:06:54 acceptable candidate for the job.

2:06:56 So, you were elected like us.

2:06:58 Yeah, that's the first time.

2:06:59 I know. We're a peer group. Okay.

2:07:02 All right. Thank you, Annika, and we

2:07:03 look forward to having you here.

2:07:06 Um, okay.

2:07:08 Next up, presentations, discussions of

2:07:11 current issues. First up, Brookline's

2:07:13 METCO program presentation by Malcolm

2:07:16 Cawthorn. Welcome, Malcolm.

2:07:29 Hello and thank you for having me today.

2:07:31 It's a

2:07:32 a privilege and an honor to be here. Um,

2:07:35 it's a privilege and honor to talk about

2:07:36 METCO this evening.

2:07:38 Hopefully I'll present

2:07:40 well enough and provide time for some

2:07:43 questions. Um,

2:07:45 I'll also say, you know, at my core I'm

2:07:47 a classroom teacher.

2:07:49 I've been interrupted by the best of

2:07:50 them.

2:07:51 But if I say something that's kind of

2:07:53 like, wait, what? Just Yeah, just stop

2:07:54 me. Um,

2:07:56 and you'll probably do it in a more

2:07:58 polite way than 16-year-olds used to.

2:08:00 Um, I do want to make a a really

2:08:03 important comment as we get ready to

2:08:04 start and um,

2:08:07 you know,

2:08:09 last week they put a marker

2:08:11 in downtown Boston to represent Ruth

2:08:14 Batson and the NAACP's 14 demands of

2:08:17 Boston Public Schools.

2:08:19 And I think as we dive into this, it's

2:08:21 really important to recognize

2:08:23 Ruth Batson, the woman who who

2:08:25 what is often referred to her as the

2:08:27 mother of METCO,

2:08:29 but also that many people look at those

2:08:31 demands today

2:08:34 and see the same fight in front of us,

2:08:37 in this case 63 years later.

2:08:39 Uh but um as we talk about METCO, we'll

2:08:41 talk more about the 60th anniversary and

2:08:43 what that means. And so I think it's

2:08:45 really important to acknowledge that,

2:08:47 you know, speaking tonight, um

2:08:50 I'm speaking from a position of carrying

2:08:53 history with me.

2:08:55 Um

2:08:57 I'm not speaking for myself.

2:08:59 I'm speaking for a program

2:09:01 uh and for a town and for things that

2:09:04 mean a lot to a lot of people.

2:09:07 And so

2:09:09 thank you for the time. Um

2:09:12 I ain't, you know, telling Betsy what to

2:09:13 do, but

2:09:15 um

2:09:16 So this is a side with some history and

2:09:17 demographics. I think it's really

2:09:19 important um that we start in

2:09:22 remembering

2:09:24 that

2:09:25 much of the crucial things that led to

2:09:28 METCO happened in what is

2:09:31 this space, not exactly in

2:09:34 um construction because this building's

2:09:36 been

2:09:37 uh

2:09:38 you know, uh renovated multiple times,

2:09:40 but

2:09:41 you know, in 1964 is Brookline residents

2:09:44 from a civil rights group that came

2:09:45 before this committee

2:09:47 and said we need to help what's going on

2:09:49 in Boston, which most people don't

2:09:51 realize, like that you know, most people

2:09:53 take that to like 1974 and Judge Garrity

2:09:56 and all those other things. No, that's

2:09:58 in 1964.

2:10:00 And so it's really important to

2:10:01 acknowledge that. It's also really

2:10:02 important in '65 that then the

2:10:03 president, now what we call the chair,

2:10:06 took action on that.

2:10:08 Created ad hoc committee, worked with

2:10:09 Boston and Brookline civil rights

2:10:11 activists to help create what would be

2:10:13 the transfer program urban to suburban

2:10:16 for broke for students to come to

2:10:18 districts like Brookline.

2:10:20 Um

2:10:21 ultimately, you know, in '66

2:10:25 uh METCO is

2:10:26 uh incorporated, files papers with the

2:10:29 state.

2:10:30 And by September 8th, 1966, it is

2:10:33 running under a grant and ultimately

2:10:35 taken over under state funding.

2:10:38 Um and the last point I want to make is

2:10:41 in 1974, that's when Brookline gets its

2:10:43 first METCO director.

2:10:45 And I point that out not because it's

2:10:47 the same year as the Judge Garrity

2:10:49 decision,

2:10:50 but some of the first riders that I've

2:10:51 been talking to, when I say rider, r i d

2:10:54 e r, people who rode the bus,

2:10:57 they talk about what that experience was

2:10:59 like

2:11:00 with nobody there for them specifically.

2:11:04 That they were told just go to school.

2:11:07 Get on that bus, get there, and go to

2:11:09 school. Nobody was there for them. And

2:11:11 Pam King, who is a civil rights activist

2:11:14 to this day, who's the daughter of Mel

2:11:15 King,

2:11:16 she often talks about how hard it would

2:11:18 have been for her to send her kids to

2:11:20 METCO.

2:11:21 And then, not to pat myself on the back

2:11:23 cuz she's speaking about me

2:11:24 metaphorically, she's saying,

2:11:25 "But it would have been different if we

2:11:26 had a Malcolm."

2:11:28 If there was a director.

2:11:31 The same year we also hired a BHS

2:11:32 coordinator. So, uh that's, you know,

2:11:35 one of the long-standing names in this

2:11:37 town through school, Gretchen Underwood,

2:11:39 is that METCO coordinator in '74. And

2:11:41 so,

2:11:43 what I hope you'll see is, whoop, over

2:11:45 time, um

2:11:47 we've really progressed with METCO as

2:11:49 well as the town.

2:11:52 Okay. I think next slide. Let's see it.

2:11:55 Currently, we have 299 students.

2:11:58 We have a student capacity that is set

2:12:00 at 303 by DESE.

2:12:02 They're in all 8 K-8 schools. Some

2:12:05 districts do that different, um where

2:12:07 they like locate them in in certain

2:12:10 schools, and they're at BHS.

2:12:13 We currently have 18 staff members, and

2:12:15 And so important to think of in that

2:12:16 last point. That includes an executive

2:12:19 assistant, a BHS coordinator,

2:12:22 and an advisor, eight K8 advisors, three

2:12:25 social workers, and three bus monitors.

2:12:28 Maintaining student capacity is what

2:12:30 maintains the program's funding from

2:12:31 DESE.

2:12:33 So, for us to continue to have the staff

2:12:35 that we need, and I'll talk more about

2:12:38 that, it is really important

2:12:40 that we keep our numbers up, or else

2:12:43 that funding will be cut, and we won't

2:12:45 be able to serve METCO students the way

2:12:46 we need to.

2:12:48 I'm not going to really read over the

2:12:49 vision cuz that's

2:12:51 I'd worry about I think one of the

2:12:52 things that is most important

2:12:54 [clears throat]

2:12:54 the goal of METCO is to bring

2:12:56 integration and diversity in a multitude

2:12:58 of ways in the 21st century.

2:13:01 That is not limited to race, which is

2:13:03 what it was originally set to do.

2:13:05 Originally, it's described as a

2:13:07 desegregation program.

2:13:09 But, METCO brings

2:13:11 a host [snorts] of

2:13:13 diverse things, and I'll point out one.

2:13:16 You know, the shifts in the town of

2:13:18 Brookline have shifted populations in a

2:13:20 lot of ways.

2:13:22 A lot of our METCO families

2:13:25 represent

2:13:27 working class, middle class, and even

2:13:29 upper middle class folks of race that

2:13:32 don't necessarily live in the town of

2:13:34 Brookline.

2:13:36 Two years ago, Boston Magazine did

2:13:38 Boston's Black 100.

2:13:40 Four Brookline METCO parents were in

2:13:42 there.

2:13:44 Not METCO parents, Brookline METCO

2:13:45 parents.

2:13:46 And so, it is really important to kind

2:13:48 of grasp that piece,

2:13:50 um, but it's religious, it's cultural,

2:13:52 it's ethnic diversity, it's all those

2:13:53 things that METCO helps bring to our

2:13:55 district.

2:13:56 Um, next slide, Betsy.

2:14:00 This is our org chart.

2:14:02 Um,

2:14:04 this took a lot of work, not the design

2:14:06 itself, but to get where we're at. Um,

2:14:09 I saw two of the prior, um, directors,

2:14:12 Dr. Susie Tulu-Dar and Dr. Keith Lezama.

2:14:15 And they were like, "We can't believe

2:14:16 you did it." I I want to give both of

2:14:18 them credit for starting the the build,

2:14:21 in particular Keith Lezama starting to

2:14:23 move to getting an advisor at every

2:14:25 school. That is something principals

2:14:28 asked for.

2:14:29 That people were splitting schools, and

2:14:32 what it meant is they had to do really

2:14:33 hard decisions.

2:14:36 There's an intense issue at this one

2:14:38 school, and then there's an IEP where

2:14:40 they need a lot of support at another

2:14:41 school. Which one am I supposed to be at

2:14:43 if they're both at two?

2:14:46 And they couldn't.

2:14:48 It has helped [clears throat] enormously

2:14:51 having an advisor at every school.

2:14:53 And I want to point out the picture

2:14:55 there. Um

2:14:57 Joanne Sylva Enjoku could not be here

2:14:58 tonight. She's dealing with um some

2:15:01 personal and family issues.

2:15:02 She's often known as the origami lady.

2:15:05 She does that for a lot of things, helps

2:15:07 to get kids to open up, also helps with

2:15:09 the small motor skills and cultural

2:15:11 learning.

2:15:12 But Joanne was one of the people to be

2:15:14 honored tonight for 30 years of service.

2:15:16 And I wanted that picture in there cuz

2:15:18 she's retiring this year as well.

2:15:21 But she will retire

2:15:23 working for the town of Brookline and

2:15:24 for half of MECO's life

2:15:26 in Brookline.

2:15:29 And so I really want to make sure,

2:15:31 knowing she couldn't be here, that we at

2:15:33 least had a chance to honor and see a

2:15:35 little bit of her and her magic with

2:15:36 children in doing origami.

2:15:40 Thank you.

2:15:43 So,

2:15:45 I can't tell you how many times we have

2:15:46 How do you end up in MECO? Or I get a

2:15:48 phone call like, "Hey, can you pull my

2:15:49 kid in MECO?" That answer is almost

2:15:51 always no.

2:15:53 But

2:15:54 to give you you

2:15:56 It's important to know MECO incorporate

2:15:57 takes every application.

2:15:59 I never receive an application.

2:16:02 And then they run their lottery.

2:16:04 That is in the midst of changing. This

2:16:06 year they ran four separate lotteries.

2:16:08 The first one was in early December, and

2:16:10 there's dates and on like that.

2:16:13 Mekko meets with PSB enrollment, we get

2:16:15 a sense of space.

2:16:17 Brooklyn MicroSchools ask for referrals

2:16:19 from Mekko in late January early

2:16:20 February based upon that meeting with

2:16:22 enrollment. And what that really is

2:16:24 about is our senior class.

2:16:26 So, we wait until after mid-year exams,

2:16:29 where we can see pretty clearly are we

2:16:31 going to get people to graduate or not?

2:16:33 And that becomes the basis of our

2:16:34 starting point, but it's not the basis

2:16:37 of our

2:16:38 full intake as hopefully you will see.

2:16:41 We meet with enrollment, we talk with

2:16:42 it, we start to look, and then we start

2:16:43 to do that. And then,

2:16:45 once we start to get referrals,

2:16:48 we are only looking at that point for

2:16:51 two referrals.

2:16:52 Referrals for kindergarten,

2:16:54 and Mekko has what they call sibling

2:16:56 preference.

2:16:58 And so, if there's a sibling in the

2:17:00 district, that person can in essence

2:17:02 jump the lottery.

2:17:04 And so, that's where we start.

2:17:08 Once we get referrals, we start to hold

2:17:10 in-person

2:17:11 um and Zoom orientations. We try to do

2:17:14 them in person. We always offer a Zoom

2:17:16 for those that can't make it.

2:17:18 Then we do what we call family intake

2:17:19 meetings, where we meet one-on-one,

2:17:21 myself and one of those social workers,

2:17:23 to work with the family. We start to

2:17:25 gain lots of information.

2:17:28 Then we host kindergarten day. That was

2:17:30 in conjunction with the BEEP program.

2:17:32 They came in, they sat in on BEEP

2:17:33 classes. We wanted to see them

2:17:35 interacting in particular without mom

2:17:36 and dad next to them.

2:17:39 And then, we start to place families.

2:17:43 And that is a bit of a puzzle.

2:17:45 Cuz again, with siblings, we want to

2:17:46 make sure they're in the same school.

2:17:50 But we only have spaces so many. So, we

2:17:51 have to like work the puzzle so that we

2:17:53 can fit the spaces that were provided at

2:17:55 those places.

2:17:57 And so, you know, that's a it takes a

2:17:59 long time, and it's um

2:18:02 I would say well worth it. It helps us

2:18:05 really get to know families, introduce

2:18:07 them to ourselves and what MECO is, and

2:18:09 then place them as best as we can within

2:18:12 our schools to be successful, which is

2:18:14 hard with a four- or five-year-old

2:18:16 projecting until they're 18.

2:18:19 But,

2:18:20 we feel like we have enough information

2:18:21 to try. So, that's where we go.

2:18:24 All right. Next slide, please.

2:18:27 Our budgeting process, um,

2:18:29 DESE holds the information for our

2:18:30 distribution of funds. It's a budget

2:18:32 line in the state budget. Um, they carve

2:18:35 it up and tell us

2:18:37 um, DESE tells uh, Brookline state grant

2:18:39 money that will be received from that

2:18:40 line item. I found that out last Friday.

2:18:45 Now, you know, they give us clues a

2:18:46 little bit ahead of time.

2:18:48 But, the official that I got was last

2:18:49 Friday, and it's probably wrong.

2:18:52 And I only say that because the governor

2:18:53 hasn't signed it.

2:18:55 And so, while they're still negotiating

2:18:57 and doing everything else,

2:18:59 I could find out in July that it's a

2:19:01 little less than what we're supposed to

2:19:02 get, or I could find July it's a little

2:19:04 more, but that's how it works. We wait

2:19:06 for them, and they tell us, and then we

2:19:07 set a budget. The budget's then

2:19:09 submitted and approved by PSB Finance.

2:19:11 It is signed by the superintendent, and

2:19:12 then sent to DESE for approval.

2:19:16 DESE limits the way MECO money can be

2:19:18 spent. Any money spent has to

2:19:21 explicitly impact Brookline MECO, the

2:19:24 Brookline MECO program, and its

2:19:25 children. Doesn't mean it can't impact

2:19:28 Brookline kids.

2:19:30 But, if it's only impacting Brookline

2:19:31 kids, we will be cited. We have been

2:19:34 cited before. Um,

2:19:36 and so, I think it's really important to

2:19:38 know

2:19:39 that if I can't make the argument that

2:19:41 it's impacting MECO kids,

2:19:43 DESE can then say, "You have to shift

2:19:45 that money."

2:19:47 This is in particular

2:19:49 This happens a lot, particularly around

2:19:50 teachers.

2:19:51 Um,

2:19:53 as far as I know, I asked Susie to look

2:19:54 that up.

2:19:55 As early as 2011, the Brookline MECO

2:19:57 program has always played for paid for

2:19:59 PSB teachers.

2:20:02 This year is five, adding up to almost

2:20:04 $600,000.

2:20:06 That we just pay for. So, I think and we

2:20:09 have to make the argument which ones fit

2:20:12 and directly impact METCO.

2:20:14 And so, I think that's really important

2:20:15 for us to know.

2:20:16 Uh next slide, please.

2:20:20 So, you guys got an annual report.

2:20:22 We have three big goals this year. Um

2:20:25 you see a lot of pictures of our babies

2:20:26 and doing a lot of things, I'll say

2:20:29 a few things. We talk about uh caregiver

2:20:30 engagement, you can see a picture from

2:20:32 the parents we honored. Um

2:20:34 you also see some different pictures of

2:20:36 them as well. Um

2:20:38 and some of the pictures of our babies,

2:20:40 um

2:20:41 that's the top middle one is me reading

2:20:44 to

2:20:45 fourth graders. I can't believe I got up

2:20:46 off the floor after that, but

2:20:49 it was a it was a beautiful day last

2:20:50 Friday, actually. I was reading poetry.

2:20:52 Two of our graduates, one of our

2:20:54 graduates from Brown.

2:20:56 Uh the ca- the rising ninth grader day

2:20:58 at BHS. A group picture of our seniors

2:21:01 with Superintendent Wong, as well as

2:21:03 Anthony Meyer, BHS coordinator.

2:21:07 Um as well as one of our

2:21:09 big projects with MIT and the Sloan

2:21:12 School.

2:21:13 The other thing we're looking at are

2:21:14 successful academic transitions. I'll

2:21:16 talk more about that. Grade eight to

2:21:18 nine is the obvious in this town, but we

2:21:20 also have a lot. We're really focusing

2:21:22 next year on a pilot grade five to six.

2:21:24 I'll talk more about that. And then, we

2:21:26 really wanted to set up METCO turning 60

2:21:28 next year, which is a big deal.

2:21:31 It is a big deal. I'll explain more as

2:21:33 we get to that slide.

2:21:34 And so, next slide.

2:21:37 You know, did we meet them?

2:21:40 That's always the hardest thing. Um

2:21:42 I think we did well.

2:21:44 I think we have more work to do, and

2:21:45 you'll see some of these are going to be

2:21:47 on repeat. Um

2:21:49 and I'll go through these quickly. You

2:21:51 know, one of the things we want to do is

2:21:51 return to pre-COVID in-person and Zoom

2:21:53 parent meetings. We have coffee talks

2:21:55 every other month on Friday with parents

2:21:58 to to zoom and see what's going on.

2:22:00 Uh we have a community family engagement

2:22:02 advisor who does events for METCO and

2:22:04 community adults.

2:22:05 Uh we established a monthly newsletter

2:22:07 for caregivers, alumni, and community

2:22:08 members. I know that goes to school

2:22:10 committee. There's folks in this room

2:22:12 that aren't getting it, please let me

2:22:13 know. We will put you on there.

2:22:15 And then we uh started exit interviews

2:22:19 or an exit questionnaire this spring.

2:22:21 Um it's for graduating eighth graders,

2:22:23 12th graders, and their caregivers to

2:22:25 give us feedback as they're leaving each

2:22:28 school.

2:22:29 Um

2:22:31 we also let look for academic

2:22:33 transitions

2:22:34 led by our learning outcomes advisor who

2:22:35 also tracks grade data.

2:22:37 You saw the picture of the BHS eighth

2:22:38 grade uh visit with eight Well, stop.

2:22:41 They visit the eighth graders at their

2:22:43 school and they bring them to the high

2:22:44 school for a day.

2:22:46 Um we have a move on up ceremony that

2:22:48 some of you were at with the rising

2:22:49 ninth graders as well as the graduating

2:22:51 12th graders. And again, starting a

2:22:53 pilot for sixth grade at

2:22:55 Driscoll to help see successes in our

2:22:57 fifth graders going into from a single

2:22:59 classroom to meeting with five teachers

2:23:02 daily.

2:23:04 And then finally, METCO 60. We're

2:23:05 launching a summer writing project. I'll

2:23:07 make sure that gets to all of you.

2:23:09 Um there'll be a reading in November. We

2:23:11 want to establish METCO uh METCO Day.

2:23:14 Brookline METCO Day is September 8th.

2:23:15 That's the first day those buses rolled

2:23:17 in 1966.

2:23:18 We'll have panel conversations with the

2:23:20 first writers as well as

2:23:22 you know, part of my privilege and honor

2:23:23 in being here. Um

2:23:26 METCO's going into its 60th year.

2:23:29 Brookline has had five METCO directors.

2:23:33 I'm the fifth.

2:23:36 In four years, I have seen districts

2:23:38 have five METCO directors.

2:23:42 And so

2:23:43 that's why it's so serious for me and

2:23:44 such an honor, but it says a lot about

2:23:46 this town, this district, and what this

2:23:48 means that we've only had five

2:23:51 in 60 years.

2:23:54 Okay.

2:23:55 Um we're also going to do a little

2:23:56 celebrating at Meco Homecoming. We're

2:23:58 playing a Meco Career Day as well as um

2:24:00 one of those panels um as well as a

2:24:02 celebration and then um in February Meco

2:24:05 celebration.

2:24:06 Um yeah.

2:24:11 So, when I think about my leadership and

2:24:12 where we're going

2:24:14 it's it's been a it's been

2:24:17 a good fruitful ride and there's been

2:24:18 things that have been really hard that I

2:24:20 don't want to dwell on, but because

2:24:22 those things have been hard,

2:24:23 establishing consistency has been hard.

2:24:25 And so, you'll hear me saying that

2:24:26 throughout these leadership and

2:24:27 directions. And one of the things is to

2:24:29 establish consistency in the Brookline

2:24:31 Meco staff and organizational structure

2:24:33 so that the Meco program grows with new

2:24:36 central admin in schools.

2:24:38 Um

2:24:39 you know

2:24:40 uh

2:24:41 it's it's been it's been complicated yet

2:24:44 fruitful as all the changes have

2:24:45 happened here as well as in the schools.

2:24:48 Um and so, we want to make sure we're

2:24:51 firm on the ground so that we can work

2:24:53 with all the changes. And part of that

2:24:55 is that advisor in every school, making

2:24:57 sure we have SEL to help cover

2:25:00 and really building the program. I think

2:25:02 we're we're done growing in that way.

2:25:05 Now it's to solidify what that means

2:25:07 and how we interact with schools and

2:25:09 central central admin.

2:25:11 We want to establish consistent

2:25:12 communication connection between Meco

2:25:14 staff, schools, caregivers, and both

2:25:16 Boston and Brookline communities. This

2:25:18 is complicated and it's one of the

2:25:19 things we'll get to a little bit more,

2:25:20 but

2:25:21 in establishing that, what what it means

2:25:24 is

2:25:24 you know, how do people know to look for

2:25:26 our information? Is it email? Is it the

2:25:28 newsletter? When we work with parents,

2:25:30 [clears throat]

2:25:31 um particularly around the bus, which oh

2:25:35 I tell Kefa Zama every time he told me

2:25:37 about the bus. I I think I don't know.

2:25:39 But what we found is texting works

2:25:42 better with parents around the bus.

2:25:44 So, we've we've been hounding for

2:25:45 numbers like, "Hey, the bus is 5 minutes

2:25:47 late." So, we text them.

2:25:48 And that's worked so much better, but

2:25:50 finding that consistent method is really

2:25:52 hard. It's taken a lot of trial and

2:25:54 error. And so, how do we do that as well

2:25:56 as thinking about what that means with

2:25:58 communicating with schools, what that

2:25:59 means with communicating with teachers,

2:26:01 all those things. Um you know, I would

2:26:03 say the great and terrible is Brookline

2:26:05 has eight wonderfully different K8s.

2:26:10 And I always thought they should be

2:26:11 different.

2:26:12 They reach very different populations.

2:26:14 They have very different setups,

2:26:15 everything else.

2:26:17 But, that means we have to figure that

2:26:18 out every time.

2:26:19 And so, you know, I I think it's a good

2:26:21 challenge, but it is a challenge.

2:26:24 Um we want to establish consistent

2:26:25 support and partnerships for students

2:26:27 and families and create paths

2:26:29 and opportunities for students to and

2:26:30 families to grow upon graduation. So,

2:26:33 one of the

2:26:34 easiest ones to point out is building

2:26:35 bridges in the college proven program,

2:26:37 which we've contracted with before for a

2:26:38 while. They do college coaching as well

2:26:41 as promote historically black colleges

2:26:42 and universities. But, this is also

2:26:44 something we share with other programs

2:26:46 at the high school in particular, Steps

2:26:47 to Success and um

2:26:50 AALSP.

2:26:51 Um

2:26:53 we want to establish accountability that

2:26:54 can be shared and merged with PSB, METCO

2:26:55 Inc., and METCO Directors Association.

2:26:58 I've said we've been on a like full

2:27:00 sprint since April break.

2:27:02 And that's not bad.

2:27:05 It's not.

2:27:06 Um

2:27:07 an example of this collaboration is

2:27:10 uh Friday when we had the May 29th when

2:27:12 we had that end of the year celebration.

2:27:15 That was also the same day as the 7th

2:27:16 and 8th grade track meet.

2:27:18 It was also the same day the METCO

2:27:19 Directors decided to do their middle

2:27:20 school retreat.

2:27:22 Yeah, we have to collaborate that

2:27:23 better.

2:27:24 [laughter]

2:27:24 We have to figure that out. And um and

2:27:27 so, yeah, I mean, it's it's just it's

2:27:29 just to continue the work in in

2:27:30 collaborating so that our kids can have

2:27:33 every opportunity cuz all three of those

2:27:34 are or

2:27:35 opportunities.

2:27:36 And we don't want our kids to miss any

2:27:38 of them.

2:27:39 But some had to because of all that, you

2:27:42 know, crossing and they just couldn't

2:27:43 get to all of them. And then the last

2:27:45 thing is establishing consistent metrics

2:27:47 to explore the Brookline METCO program's

2:27:49 effectiveness.

2:27:50 So,

2:27:51 that's program effectiveness, that's

2:27:52 student

2:27:54 performance, that's all kinds of things.

2:27:56 Um

2:27:57 And I think we're now at a point where

2:27:58 we can really do that. We've hit the

2:27:59 strength, we've hit the met and we're

2:28:01 trying to figure out what are those

2:28:02 consistent met metrics that we use every

2:28:04 year.

2:28:06 Next slide.

2:28:11 So, data collection.

2:28:13 I think one of the things that's really

2:28:14 important in this is a chance to see

2:28:15 METCO in its distinct light.

2:28:18 I think what happens a lot with METCO is

2:28:20 um we get lumped in a combination with

2:28:23 other racial groups or other programs.

2:28:25 And so,

2:28:27 we've worked really hard to try and

2:28:28 separate. I would say METCO Incorporated

2:28:30 has as well.

2:28:32 Um

2:28:33 in that presentation there's a link to

2:28:34 the executive summary of the Seton

2:28:36 report that was commissioned by uh METCO

2:28:39 Inc.

2:28:40 that went from really

2:28:42 2002 to 2020.

2:28:45 Um

2:28:46 it was a really long study. It looked at

2:28:48 a lot of different things. I won't go

2:28:49 into it, but that was done through METCO

2:28:51 Inc. to help show the effectiveness of

2:28:52 METCO. We also have a learning outcomes

2:28:54 advisor, Mr. Loren Green.

2:28:57 He was hired by that um under the past

2:28:59 uh director and I have kept him. He is

2:29:03 his value to us is hard to actually

2:29:05 measure

2:29:06 um

2:29:07 because he looks specifically at

2:29:08 Brookline METCO data.

2:29:10 And so, he helps in so many ways to help

2:29:13 paint a picture of what METCO is doing

2:29:15 and what we're doing well and where we

2:29:17 need work and where we need to work with

2:29:18 the schools.

2:29:19 So,

2:29:20 the next bullet, METCO students on

2:29:21 average have a higher GPA than resident

2:29:24 black and Latinx students. That's from

2:29:26 Green's research and his data collection

2:29:28 and along with what was then called the

2:29:30 BHS data team, which I don't know if

2:29:31 that

2:29:32 is still called that.

2:29:34 [laughter]

2:29:34 But, they had a data team that was

2:29:35 looking and they were starting to find,

2:29:37 "Wow, like METCO kids score really well

2:29:40 in comparison to things that they often

2:29:41 lumped into."

2:29:43 Um

2:29:45 99.97%

2:29:46 of Brookline METCO students graduate

2:29:48 from BHS.

2:29:50 We have had one student not graduate

2:29:54 since the turn of the century.

2:29:57 And [gasps and sighs] so, that says a

2:29:58 lot.

2:29:59 80% attend four-year college.

2:30:02 That mostly comes from the Satcher and

2:30:04 Report as well as some other things. And

2:30:05 then, we want to use Brookline METCO

2:30:07 data to identify gaps and problematic

2:30:09 data points within PSB and Brookline

2:30:11 METCO. And so, part of the reason we

2:30:13 talked a lot about transitions, we were

2:30:15 noticing some really

2:30:17 disturbing dips.

2:30:19 Kids doing very well in certain subjects

2:30:21 in eighth grade, then all of a sudden in

2:30:22 ninth grade not performing well.

2:30:24 And so, our goal is not to point fingers

2:30:27 at that.

2:30:28 It's to be in a collaborative mode to

2:30:30 work with

2:30:32 whatever's happening in eighth grade and

2:30:34 helping ninth grade and fix that

2:30:35 transition so we can see something

2:30:37 that's similar in performance.

2:30:40 Okay, next slide.

2:30:44 Family and community engagement. We have

2:30:45 a family community engagement advisor,

2:30:47 her name is Vina Harvey.

2:30:49 A lot of people know her cuz she seems

2:30:51 to be the face. She's the one who's

2:30:52 always running around getting table

2:30:53 cloths and like, you know, cuz she's

2:30:55 running all the community stuff.

2:30:57 But, I think what is missed in that is

2:30:58 how hard

2:31:00 she works from not just METCO to look

2:31:02 good, but also to build bonds beyond

2:31:05 just within METCO.

2:31:07 And so, some of the family and community

2:31:08 events

2:31:09 for students and families, we have

2:31:10 roller skating, we have bowling, we have

2:31:12 family fun day that was just last

2:31:13 Saturday. For adults, we do the It Takes

2:31:15 a Village. We have our gala. And then,

2:31:17 we try to give back. We do the METCO

2:31:19 appreciation breakfast at BHS.

2:31:22 And we do Janet Morse Awards to

2:31:23 recognize caregivers who help so often.

2:31:27 We also try to link METCO to BHS and

2:31:29 Brookline communities. So, we invite all

2:31:31 PSB students to the roller skating party

2:31:32 in Dorchester.

2:31:34 And some come, some don't, but it's an

2:31:35 invitation. Come on out. Um It Takes a

2:31:38 Village is an opportunity, some of you

2:31:40 were there, to meet with school

2:31:41 committee, central office, school

2:31:42 leadership. It's a chance to build those

2:31:44 bonds outside of town and school

2:31:47 buildings.

2:31:48 And then we also do the G. McGuire

2:31:49 Awards, where we recognize PSB staff

2:31:52 for all that they do for our students

2:31:54 and PSB.

2:31:56 And then, how do we measure the METCO

2:31:58 family experience? So, we talked about

2:31:59 the exit questionnaires. That's new this

2:32:01 spring.

2:32:02 Um I do want to say one thing. I

2:32:04 purposely

2:32:05 don't have information on that yet,

2:32:07 mostly cuz they grads haven't graduated,

2:32:09 but also I wanted parents and kids to

2:32:12 say what they wanted to say.

2:32:14 And it doesn't come to me. It goes to

2:32:16 Lauren Green.

2:32:18 And part of the reason for that is that

2:32:20 I And I told parents was like, "I want

2:32:21 you to feel free to say If you have to

2:32:22 critique me, let's do it."

2:32:25 You guys can Let's do it. So, that way

2:32:27 they're not worried about saying things

2:32:29 about me and it coming to me directly.

2:32:31 Um

2:32:33 we did the revival parent meetings, and

2:32:35 we reestablished the Brookline METCO

2:32:36 parent group.

2:32:39 Bless you.

2:32:40 Next slide, please.

2:32:44 So,

2:32:45 well, the goal to have the uh annual

2:32:47 report is by July 31st. Part of that is

2:32:49 because we need final grades.

2:32:51 Um

2:32:52 and we need some time to assess some of

2:32:54 those things, but one of the things we

2:32:55 really want to do, and this is what I'm

2:32:57 anticipating,

2:32:59 um we'll put it into firm language in

2:33:02 the um annual report. We want to do a

2:33:03 closer examine of attendance and its

2:33:05 correlation to academic SEL performance,

2:33:08 and working with our schools for earlier

2:33:09 intervention on poor attendance.

2:33:12 Um

2:33:13 you know, and again, the idea is working

2:33:14 with them, cuz I think sometimes that

2:33:16 gets lost in the shuffle, particularly

2:33:17 when METCO provides a bus.

2:33:19 Right? Whose responsibility is it for

2:33:21 attendance? Well, it's all of our

2:33:22 responsibility. So, how do we coordinate

2:33:24 that? We're going to develop

2:33:26 district-wide plan for Meco grade 5

2:33:27 through 6 transition, which I talked a

2:33:29 little bit about. Um

2:33:31 you know, that move from single

2:33:32 classroom into multiple classrooms and

2:33:34 what that means, as well as beginning to

2:33:36 explore data-informed transition for

2:33:38 grade 2 through 2 through 3. One of the

2:33:41 really interesting things, um

2:33:43 I talked about a little bit of dip in

2:33:44 9th grade. What we see though for Meco

2:33:46 kids is they get progressively better

2:33:48 over high school.

2:33:51 So, when you look at a a group's grades

2:33:52 in 9th grade, that group looks better in

2:33:55 10th, and they look better

2:33:57 again in 11th and in 12th.

2:34:01 One of my theories, and I'm going to

2:34:03 make it a theory, is that there's

2:34:05 actually less transitions in high

2:34:06 school.

2:34:09 The schedule stays the same. You have

2:34:11 the same guidance counselor. You have

2:34:13 the same dean. You have the same Meco

2:34:15 staff.

2:34:16 That's not true in the K8s.

2:34:19 It shifts a lot. So, I'm not saying that

2:34:22 it I know that's what we have to do,

2:34:23 right? And so, what my our goal is to

2:34:26 look at how can we help our kids

2:34:27 anticipate that and our families

2:34:29 anticipate that, move from Google

2:34:30 Classroom to Canvas.

2:34:34 And as a teacher who taught at the high

2:34:35 school, knowing

2:34:37 Canvas can be easily manipulated by

2:34:39 smart young people when they want to.

2:34:41 Had a lot of talks with parents about

2:34:43 well, he said he turned it in and I look

2:34:45 yeah anyway, how to help that?

2:34:48 We have to look at 5 6 as well as 2

2:34:49 through 3.

2:34:51 And then the last thing, which I already

2:34:52 meant, that balanced calendar

2:34:54 with the schools so our kids and

2:34:57 families can have the riches of

2:34:59 everything.

2:35:00 Uh next slide.

2:35:04 So, Meco Meco 60, we're going to theme

2:35:06 60 years of excellence, solidarity and

2:35:08 tradition has created thousands of

2:35:09 diamonds.

2:35:11 I would ask that you please consider

2:35:12 writing.

2:35:14 We're doing a summer writing project.

2:35:15 We're looking for community members.

2:35:17 Um also, when it comes in September,

2:35:19 purchasing some Meco 60 merch.

2:35:22 It will go to the Meco 60 stuff, but you

2:35:24 know, a lot of people see the stuff

2:35:26 around town like, "Oh, how do I get a

2:35:27 Meco sweatshirt?" That's one way.

2:35:29 Um or attending at least one event. Um

2:35:32 full finalized calendars will be

2:35:34 distributed August 1st.

2:35:36 Um please help us get September 8th to

2:35:38 be recognized as Meco Day. I've been

2:35:40 meeting with town officials. We're going

2:35:41 to try and go through town meeting in

2:35:42 the fall.

2:35:43 We feel like it's important after 60

2:35:45 years for the town.

2:35:47 Every September 8th is Meco Day.

2:35:49 And I don't know what that means beyond

2:35:51 saying that.

2:35:53 But sometimes you just need a stamp for

2:35:56 posterity.

2:35:58 Cuz there'll be a kid long after all of

2:35:59 us are here and be like, "What's Meco 60

2:36:01 What's that? Meco Day?" Yeah, that's the

2:36:03 first day the writers came

2:36:04 in 1966. Here's that story.

2:36:07 And then, please support us in getting

2:36:10 We're looking at um a filmmaker and a

2:36:12 historian to document Brookline Meco's

2:36:13 history and our narrative. I think

2:36:15 that's really important. I think when I

2:36:17 hear a lot of um

2:36:20 information that kind of comes jumbled

2:36:22 or is confusing for me,

2:36:24 it's because um

2:36:27 uh Meco has been very good at like

2:36:30 uh supporting and pushing out and and

2:36:33 and protecting and [clears throat]

2:36:34 advocating for our students and

2:36:36 families.

2:36:37 Uh as an old history teacher, I don't

2:36:39 think we've been great at establishing

2:36:41 what Meco is

2:36:42 and then saying what it is and then

2:36:44 having a document to say what it is and

2:36:46 then having a document in every library

2:36:47 to say what it is.

2:36:49 And so, this is stuff we're really

2:36:51 thinking of doing. You know, we talked

2:36:52 about the writing, that's going to be

2:36:53 one thing, but we really want something

2:36:55 that's documentary

2:36:57 that can be online somewhere and every

2:36:59 teacher can pull it up, as well as uh

2:37:01 having a historian write something.

2:37:04 All right.

2:37:06 And so, final thoughts. Um

2:37:10 you know, I think

2:37:12 Meco provides Brookline a chance to

2:37:13 stand tall in unison about our

2:37:14 commitment to METCO.

2:37:16 Um

2:37:18 and it is an opportunity

2:37:20 that, you know, I think, you know, we

2:37:22 have to take.

2:37:24 I think it's a chance for

2:37:25 Brookline to separate itself from other

2:37:26 districts for inclusion and excellence

2:37:29 in our commitment to METCO.

2:37:30 There's 33 METCO districts tied to

2:37:32 Boston. There's another six tied to

2:37:33 Springfield.

2:37:35 When you ask, most are envious of

2:37:37 Brookline.

2:37:40 There's a reason for that. There's a lot

2:37:41 that goes into that. I think we have a

2:37:43 chance to separate ourselves in our

2:37:45 greatness.

2:37:48 There's a chance to be leaders amongst

2:37:49 Boston METCO and suburban Brookline

2:37:51 partnerships. Some people are in here

2:37:53 today because we've had great

2:37:54 relationships. So, with Baffin and

2:37:56 Brookline, METCO Cares has been a really

2:37:59 good one. About 10% [clears throat] of

2:38:01 our students are identified as Asian A A

2:38:04 A H N P I. Um and so, working with

2:38:09 Baffin has been great relationship.

2:38:11 We've had a great relationship with

2:38:12 Temple Ohabei Shalom. Um in particular

2:38:14 uh they did after school care for us. We

2:38:17 still have kids who go there for after

2:38:18 school care. We couldn't actually supply

2:38:20 it fiscally. It wasn't making sense

2:38:22 anymore.

2:38:23 Um but kids, particularly from Ridley

2:38:25 and Lawrence, still go. As well as they

2:38:27 hosted our first um queer kids of color

2:38:30 unity conference.

2:38:32 And so, that all comes from a lot of

2:38:34 work with us and, you know, I was

2:38:36 fortunate enough to to speak at at

2:38:39 Temple Ohabei Shalom for their 180th

2:38:41 anniversary in my first year as METCO

2:38:43 director to talk about black and Jewish

2:38:45 relations. So, I think that's a

2:38:46 beautiful relationship that we have.

2:38:49 Um Brookline METCO and for the culture

2:38:52 um particularly tied to Juneteenth and

2:38:54 other things is something that's really

2:38:56 powerful for us.

2:38:57 Um

2:38:59 and Brookline METCO and Brookline Muslim

2:39:00 friends is something that we build on.

2:39:02 Um

2:39:03 and and have worked to really establish

2:39:06 But again, the chance to be leaders then

2:39:08 to be a shining beacon

2:39:09 and be the example for other districts.

2:39:13 And last, um

2:39:15 we want the chance to build an alumni

2:39:16 network for Brookline METCO.

2:39:19 Somebody I went to school with, Laura

2:39:21 Nelson,

2:39:22 is an incredible political strategist. I

2:39:24 say incredible like led the campaigns

2:39:27 for Elizabeth Warren, Ed Markey,

2:39:29 Ayanna Pressley.

2:39:31 She's a Brookline METCO grad.

2:39:34 The current um I'm going to forget the

2:39:36 name. [clears throat]

2:39:38 Uh is for community engagement for Mayor

2:39:40 Wu is a Brookline METCO grad.

2:39:43 And we have the I mean there's so many

2:39:45 more. I can go into the cool stuff like

2:39:47 a guy graduated Jay Pope who makes music

2:39:49 for Dr. Dre and everybody like

2:39:51 like I mean it for real. Like we have a

2:39:53 huge network and so I I would need your

2:39:55 help.

2:39:57 Cuz I know a lot of people that been

2:39:58 around for a long time, but I don't know

2:39:59 everybody

2:40:01 and I don't have all the resources. So,

2:40:03 you know, we have a chance to build a

2:40:06 again, it'll make us all look good.

2:41:44 of um

2:41:45 MECO program in Brookline that

2:41:50 I think it is different than another

2:42:01 in in leadership and guidance. The other

2:42:20 and so it all

2:42:24 there is a lot of potential here and we

2:42:31 being the standard.

2:42:40 um not just for the students, but these

2:42:45 was like the big gala, the little galas

2:42:48 um and that Vina Harvey

2:42:51 Vina Harvey, yeah.

2:42:52 Vina Harvey has has created

2:42:56 I've been in so many districts where

2:42:58 they try to create events and and and

2:43:00 people don't show up. People don't feel

2:43:03 like they're part of a community. And so

2:43:05 here, it's it's it's a big deal. People

2:43:09 come out and they enjoy each other and

2:43:11 there's this

2:43:12 amazing culture.

2:43:14 So, I've never seen anything like that

2:43:16 in other districts. It's really powerful

2:43:18 here. And as I've said, one of the

2:43:21 things about Brookline is geographically

2:43:23 where we're located. We're we're

2:43:26 bordered by Boston and so it we tap into

2:43:33 all are definitely a suburb and and we

2:43:36 are resourced. And so we end up with a

2:43:43 the METCO METCO program reflects that as

2:43:46 Um but we there's a lot I feel like

2:44:19 just, you know, we stand on the

2:48:35 Um

2:48:36 but one of the women who graduated in

2:49:26 do

2:49:27 do all of our METCO students start in

2:49:31 kindergarten or there times when a

2:49:34 student comes in in a different grade?

2:49:36 The overwhelming majority of our

2:49:37 students start in kindergarten. Um and

2:49:40 that comes from

2:49:41 graduation. Like this year we had a

2:49:43 smaller class cuz it's all by space. We

2:49:44 have 20 kids. And so

2:49:47 you know, we have close to 20

2:49:48 kindergartners coming in.

2:49:50 Uh what makes it a little tricky, and I

2:49:51 always say like, you know,

2:49:53 intake for METCO is is more of a puzzle

2:49:55 than like a lottery. So

2:49:58 um

2:50:00 we had a couple families who then had

2:50:02 older siblings, right? And so

2:50:05 we don't promise them that, but we'll

2:50:06 say, "Listen, we think we can, you know,

2:50:08 and I and I go and work with enrollment

2:50:10 and say, "Hey,

2:50:11 you know, do we have a space if we took

2:50:13 this kindergartner or second grader?" We

2:50:15 try to keep them very young so that they

2:50:17 can matriculate all the way through.

2:50:19 And we also get funky stuff. Like

2:50:22 uh you know, kids where, you know,

2:50:25 a kid's in private school, their kid

2:50:26 gets into METCO, and then later on

2:50:28 they're like, "Yeah, we like Brookline

2:50:28 better than the private school. Do you

2:50:29 have space?" And they have sibling

2:50:31 preference, so they jump the lottery,

2:50:32 but it's still based upon space. So I

2:50:34 can't just place least kid, right? Like

2:50:36 they have to have space. and then it

2:50:37 gets tricky cuz we want to keep them

2:50:38 together.

2:50:40 And so if that school doesn't have that

2:50:42 space, you know, it can be tricky, but

2:50:44 yeah, I mean, we don't Yeah, I mean

2:50:48 Mostly K.

2:50:49 It's it's overwhelming majority

2:50:50 kindergarten.

2:51:24 Um and I wonder if we are tracking data

2:51:27 on whether

2:51:29 we have students who leave Brookline in

2:51:31 favor of other

2:51:32 other METCO districts?

2:51:34 Yeah.

2:51:34 So, that's actually really hard to do.

2:51:37 METCO Inc. prohibits that cuz otherwise

2:51:39 they're shopping, right? Like and

2:51:42 unfortunately, we get most of the

2:51:43 shopping.

2:51:45 We're all sold out, but we get most of

2:51:46 the shopping.

2:51:47 So, like I had a I had a parent who was

2:51:49 at Newton and came [clears throat] to me

2:51:51 was like, "Yeah, we live in Eastie.

2:51:53 Brookline's closer.

2:51:55 Can we get into Brookline?" And I had to

2:51:56 say no.

2:51:57 Cuz like you're already in a METCO

2:51:58 district. So, they can't really shop. Um

2:52:05 I'm trying to say this.

2:52:08 So, I will say

2:52:10 in the history of METCO, there have been

2:52:12 times kids have been moved from a

2:52:13 district to another.

2:52:15 The last administration really put a

2:52:17 squash on that. Um

2:52:20 I

2:52:22 I I worry that that

2:52:24 um

2:52:25 pigeonholes when there's some really

2:52:26 glaring issues with a kid in the

2:52:28 district. Um and I understand why cuz

2:52:31 everybody would jockey and try to swap

2:52:33 and do everything else. So, it's really

2:52:35 tricky for Meiko Inc. to handle that,

2:52:37 but I would never handle that. That's

2:52:39 what I'm saying, like even though I get

2:52:40 phone calls. I want to be

2:52:42 Hey, my kids in Weston, the trip is

2:52:44 killing them. Can I come to Brookline?

2:52:46 I'm more concerned that like the kids

2:52:48 are leaving Brookline because this isn't

2:52:50 Well, they don't leave. They can't go to

2:52:52 another Meiko district. We do have

2:52:54 So, you know, we do the graduation rate

2:52:56 and there's a rate of attrition. I would

2:52:57 say the greater pool has been um

2:53:02 as kids get older, athletics in certain

2:53:04 schools in certain sports.

2:53:07 Mhm.

2:53:08 I think that's been the thing I have

2:53:10 seen the most in my four years. Um

2:53:13 but uh you know, again, we probably lose

2:53:17 four to seven kids a year and a lot of

2:53:20 that is is is moving for different

2:53:23 things. So, you know, family moves out

2:53:25 of Boston into a suburban district. Um

2:53:28 sometimes totally another state. Like we

2:53:30 had a family we loved and they're moving

2:53:32 to Rhode Island next year.

2:53:33 And so, you have to be a Boston resident

2:53:36 to be in it. But I I would say we're not

2:53:39 we don't lose very many.

2:53:41 I'll just say that. Most of our kids and

2:53:43 you hear them talk, they're

2:53:44 generational. Some of you guys met some

2:53:46 of those parents, like

2:53:47 you know, um

2:53:50 I guess I should be proud, but you know,

2:53:51 as I get older, it's harder to swallow.

2:53:53 I mean, I've taught Meiko kids, I've

2:53:55 taught their parents and their kids.

2:53:57 [laughter]

2:53:58 And and I was like, oh, I I think I went

2:53:59 to school with your mom. Like so, like

2:54:02 they keep coming back. Like like we

2:54:03 don't lose many kids that way.

2:54:05 Okay, that's great.

2:54:06 Yeah.

2:54:07 And I had two more questions. Can you

2:54:09 talk a little bit about

2:54:12 you mentioned um I think it was on the

2:54:14 the first slide, reducing racial

2:54:16 isolation. This was also in your annual

2:54:18 report. So, when you're talking about

2:54:19 reducing racial isolation, what does

2:54:21 that mean?

2:54:22 Okay.

2:54:24 Yeah.

2:54:24 of your goal. will

2:54:25 say it's it's two-fold and that's also

2:54:26 METCO Inc's vision as well. It's not

2:54:28 just mine or Brookline's. Um

2:54:31 you know,

2:54:33 we try to make sure like when we place

2:54:35 them in kindergarten that we're not

2:54:35 placing just one child.

2:54:37 That there's at least, you know, there

2:54:39 and every school is different. Some

2:54:40 school has

2:54:41 uh more racial diversity than others,

2:54:43 but no matter what, we try to make sure

2:54:45 there's at least another METCO kid who

2:54:46 understands like the journey to get

2:54:48 there, whether their parents drive them

2:54:49 in the morning or they take the bus or

2:54:50 what have you.

2:54:52 Um and so I think about that in terms of

2:54:55 how we look at the placement in that

2:54:57 puzzle.

2:54:58 But I think it's really important to

2:55:00 point out like that

2:55:01 racial isolation is also to bring

2:55:03 diversity so that kids in towns that are

2:55:09 not even as diverse as ours

2:55:11 don't live in racial isolation of

2:55:13 whiteness.

2:55:15 And so bringing kids here

2:55:18 [clears throat]

2:55:18 gives some kids an experience

2:55:20 that they might not have ever got if

2:55:22 they didn't have METCO. Um in particular

2:55:24 uh State Rep uh Chris Rowell talks

2:55:27 Rowell's talks a lot of um Rowell,

2:55:29 sorry, talks a lot about his experience

2:55:32 at Lincoln-Sudbury.

2:55:33 And now that he's in politics, but his

2:55:35 wrestling co-captain is now the mayor of

2:55:37 Lynn.

2:55:38 And they boost each other up, and that's

2:55:39 a result of that METCO racial about like

2:55:42 if they didn't wrestle together and they

2:55:44 weren't in the same grade and all they

2:55:45 never would have had that relationship.

2:55:47 And so it it really works twofold. I

2:55:49 will also say um DESE made a visit to us

2:55:52 this fall.

2:55:54 Um

2:55:55 and they met with my staff, then they

2:55:56 met with um

2:55:58 Superintendent Wong and myself and Dr.

2:56:01 Pernoit. And um

2:56:04 and we almost got in argument. They're

2:56:05 like, "I don't really believe your kids

2:56:06 feel that comfortable here."

2:56:08 I was like, "I don't know what to tell

2:56:09 you." Like, you know,

2:56:11 [laughter]

2:56:11 like we're not getting that report back.

2:56:12 And then they met with the kids. And

2:56:14 they met um

2:56:16 We brought kids from um

2:56:18 middle school and high school.

2:56:20 And I went back in to kind of follow up

2:56:22 after the day and they're like

2:56:24 they really do feel comfortable here.

2:56:26 And I was like, yeah. Like I mean, you

2:56:27 know, it's not perfect. It's not all of

2:56:28 them. They had a sample size.

2:56:30 But in general, they do. And I would

2:56:33 say, you know, we have a lot of METCO

2:56:34 kids who

2:56:36 work like, you know, that the extreme is

2:56:38 they pretty much live in Brookline.

2:56:40 Spend all their time here. Their parent

2:56:42 I see them at Pino's at 10:00. Like,

2:56:43 what are you doing? Like, oh, I was over

2:56:45 so-and-so's house. We were doing

2:56:46 homework and then we played video games

2:56:47 and we had homework and now I'm catching

2:56:48 the T, but I'm grabbing a slice from

2:56:49 Pino's on my way home.

2:56:51 And then we have others where it's

2:56:52 really functional. Like they come to go

2:56:54 to school, they go back, they go to

2:56:55 community and work. There's a whole lot

2:56:57 in between.

2:56:58 Whether it's music, drama, sports,

2:57:01 debate, uh the Cypress, like you know,

2:57:03 all those things and we try to push all

2:57:05 our METCO kids into all of them.

2:57:07 And so

2:57:08 yeah, most have a real connection

2:57:10 through something here.

2:57:12 Um and usually that is more than just a

2:57:14 friend. It's with their family.

2:57:16 It's it's connections and other things,

2:57:18 so.

2:57:18 Thank you. My last question was about

2:57:21 special education and um

2:57:23 what challenges and opportunities you

2:57:25 see.

2:57:26 Yeah.

2:57:27 Well, I think um

2:57:31 Well, I think a few things. I think it's

2:57:32 really important as we

2:57:34 um talk about special education and we

2:57:35 talk about like some of those data

2:57:37 points of making sure we're looking at

2:57:39 METCO in a distinct light as opposed to

2:57:41 like

2:57:42 well, this many black kids are getting

2:57:43 in and think that's METCO empirically.

2:57:46 Um I think two, it's really important to

2:57:49 note that um

2:57:52 you know,

2:57:53 we've lived in a really tough time these

2:57:55 last few years and I mean that in a lot

2:57:56 of ways. I'm not talking politically.

2:57:57 I'm talking about social-emotionally

2:57:59 coming out of a pandemic.

2:58:01 And I think what happens a lot

2:58:04 with the best-natured and well-meaning

2:58:06 as well as highly educated and

2:58:08 thoughtful people, we look for answers

2:58:10 to help kids be successful.

2:58:12 And so I think when we look at

2:58:15 you know, METCO kids in special

2:58:16 education, we're trying to help them be

2:58:17 successful.

2:58:19 Schools are worried about them. Teachers

2:58:20 are worried about them. Their parents

2:58:21 are worried about them. The MEKO

2:58:23 advisors are worried about them. And so,

2:58:24 we look for answers, and one of those

2:58:26 answers is special education. Um I I you

2:58:29 know, I don't want to go down the path

2:58:30 of like

2:58:32 what happens where and all these other

2:58:33 things.

2:58:34 I I think most of it is good natured and

2:58:37 really trying to help and provide

2:58:38 answers. And I think in that

2:58:41 sometimes that zeal

2:58:44 you know, shows up in things like DESI

2:58:45 and other you know, it shows up in those

2:58:47 things, and that's kind of um

2:58:52 well, it's not out of our control, but I

2:58:53 think it's it's it's without um

2:58:57 sort of boundary in the attempt to try

2:59:00 and help. And I think what um

2:59:03 we found particularly in these last few

2:59:04 years is that, you know, kids have been

2:59:06 presenting in really weird ways. I was

2:59:07 telling somebody at the high school, I

2:59:08 was like, you aren't going to get a

2:59:09 ninth grader that looks the same for a

2:59:11 long time.

2:59:13 Because this one had COVID in third

2:59:15 grade, and this one had it in sixth

2:59:16 grade, and this one had it. And so, what

2:59:17 they meant like our seniors who just

2:59:19 graduated, some of them missed half of

2:59:21 seventh grade and all of eighth grade in

2:59:22 person.

2:59:24 And so, that presents really differently

2:59:25 than a kid who walks in the ninth grade

2:59:27 now. And so, I think as we try to find

2:59:29 resolutions

2:59:30 we just have to continue to continue to

2:59:31 work and improve. So, there was a huge

2:59:33 meeting in here to talk about

2:59:34 multi-tiered, you know, systems of

2:59:36 support, those interventions, those

2:59:38 types of things that are systemic.

2:59:41 They're great, and they're going to help

2:59:42 they're going to help our MEKO kids. And

2:59:43 I think everybody's working

2:59:45 fantastically to help our MEKO kids.

2:59:47 Bella.

2:59:48 So, I think Malcolm is named our

2:59:51 findings of disproportionality.

2:59:54 And to understand for a student to be

2:59:56 eligible for IEP services, they need to

2:59:59 be they need to have a learning

3:00:01 disability. So, statistically

3:00:04 um not our students that are black and

3:00:09 and Latino are being disproportionately

3:00:11 identified for IEP. So, that's not the

3:00:14 fault of the students.

3:00:15 No.

3:00:16 That is what Malcolm is saying people

3:00:18 may be assigning them to get the extra

3:00:20 support. And that's where MTSS is the

3:00:23 answer.

3:00:24 Yeah.

3:00:24 MTSS is our route to make sure that

3:00:27 we're offering robust general

3:00:29 intervention services for students. The

3:00:32 students likely statistically do not

3:00:34 have learning disabilities at the rate

3:00:37 that they're being identified for IEP

3:00:39 services. But, that is a retraining

3:00:43 retraining of the way we're approaching

3:00:45 to make sure students get the supports

3:00:47 they need. But, we feel very good about

3:00:50 that direction that we're adopting.

3:00:53 Um so, so that's yes.

3:00:56 Yeah.

3:00:57 There is um I did want to say more about

3:00:59 that visit. So, what happened was it was

3:01:04 DESE, Darlene Fernandez, and she had a

3:01:07 goal of visiting every METCO program.

3:01:10 Yeah.

3:01:11 Every single one. So, by the time she

3:01:13 got to us, I think she was more than

3:01:15 halfway through.

3:01:16 Yeah.

3:01:16 And she was a bit jaded in in her

3:01:19 perspective. But, then she met

3:01:21 everybody.

3:01:22 Mhm.

3:01:22 And she actually apologized. She said,

3:01:24 "You know what?

3:01:26 It really is different. The students do

3:01:28 speak differently about how they are

3:01:31 included."

3:01:31 Yeah.

3:01:32 So, that that actually was is a really

3:01:34 important metric.

3:01:36 Yeah.

3:05:57 No, no, no.

3:06:07 Yes.

3:06:09 20%?

3:06:10 So, it doesn't mean they don't have a

3:06:11 plan. My It just a you know it and

3:06:15 so much depends on that kid and what we

3:06:16 can get. So, um

3:06:18 one of our toughest cases, uh a kid who

3:06:21 graduated last year, you know, um

3:06:24 uh

3:06:27 Ali White Bomb mentioned OFC, you know,

3:06:29 you know, most people know the program

3:06:30 is now ACE. It's a different program.

3:06:32 And I don't I don't mean that with any

3:06:34 slight. It's a different program. We had

3:06:36 a kid who, you know,

3:06:38 we literally kept his schedule on the

3:06:40 wall in the MECO space.

3:06:42 And he would come down there and like,

3:06:44 "Oh, this is where I'm supposed to be?"

3:06:45 And I'd be like, "Yeah, go. Get to

3:06:46 class."

3:06:48 He was into uh all kinds of things with

3:06:50 agriculture and other stuff. Now doing

3:06:52 urban farming in Boston and doing great.

3:06:55 And we hooked him up with that, not just

3:06:56 with with MECO, but that was in

3:06:57 collaboration with ACE. So, I you know,

3:07:00 to use your terminology, Bob, we try to

3:07:02 make sure kids have a dignified step.

3:07:05 Um

3:07:07 And And that can be a lot of things for

3:07:09 different kids. Uh a student you

3:07:10 probably remember who I I think of

3:07:12 dearly, I'm

3:07:13 uh you know,

3:07:14 oh, Dallas Jeter, who was struggled

3:07:16 through high school, went to the

3:07:18 military, came back, used the GI Bill,

3:07:19 and now is working on a master's at

3:07:21 Suffolk University. Like he had a

3:07:23 dignified step when he left Brookline

3:07:25 High, and that's

3:07:27 that's what we can do. We also have a

3:07:28 saying, you graduate from Brookline

3:07:30 High, but you're MECO for life.

3:07:32 Kids come back. Oh, Mr. Corthorn,

3:07:36 yeah, I got into the party life at

3:07:37 college. I need some help. I got to

3:07:38 transfer to to to to and we try to hook

3:07:40 them up and get them settled so they can

3:07:41 come back,

3:07:43 you know, make their parents a little

3:07:44 happier. Like, you know, but um you

3:07:47 know, sometimes that's when they're 18

3:07:48 and sometimes when they're 22. They're

3:07:50 like, "Yeah, I'm still trying to figure

3:07:51 it out."

3:07:52 No, I think [clears throat] we have to

3:07:53 think about the 80% and the 20%.

3:07:54 Absolutely.

3:07:55 And I guess final question. You have a

3:07:58 fairly robust staff.

3:08:00 Yes.

3:08:00 Meco staff which when I was working in

3:08:02 Brookline we didn't have that many

3:08:03 No.

3:08:04 at all. So,

3:08:09 um

3:08:10 and you look at the at the work that the

3:08:28 The Meco advisors.

3:08:30 Sure. So,

3:08:32 one of the things I say this is the

3:08:33 first year we've been able to have an

3:08:35 advisor at every school.

3:08:37 Um and um again, that was something

3:08:40 asked by the principals. So, we're we're

3:08:41 really going to have to look at that and

3:08:43 see what we can do. I think what it has

3:08:44 mitigated

3:08:46 that I can say with a lot of confidence

3:08:48 is having an advisor in every school

3:08:51 has so many that attendance issue

3:08:54 where where there were kids floating

3:08:55 around in particular, we had kids at

3:08:58 some schools who only wanted to see the

3:08:59 Meco advisor. Well, if the Meco advisor

3:09:01 wasn't there because they were at their

3:09:02 other school,

3:09:04 it was hard to get that kid regulated

3:09:05 and get back in.

3:09:07 Um and so, it has I

3:09:11 I I wish I could show you my text chain

3:09:13 from last year to this year.

3:09:17 me on a regular basis like in emergency
