3:09:22 has shrunk immensely.

3:09:24 Okay.

3:09:24 But I don't think it's my work. I think

3:09:26 it's

3:09:27 the folks who are there. And so, I think

3:09:29 that that's the easy data that I can

3:09:31 come up with. We're going to have to

3:09:32 look at the data in terms around in

3:09:34 terms of what we get and see what that

3:09:36 means. I think it has made um

3:09:41 something for me to evaluate because,

3:09:44 you know,

3:09:46 you know, you know, having a person is

3:09:47 money. Like, that's money that could go

3:09:49 to something else. I think one of the

3:09:51 complicated things in our system and is

3:09:53 please don't forget remember I love the

3:09:55 way our system is. I believe in K

3:09:56 through 8. It is why I moved back to put

3:09:58 my children through K through 8. I think

3:10:00 middle school, whoever created that

3:10:02 should

3:10:04 I don't know who thought putting the

3:10:06 most hormonally imbalanced children in

3:10:07 one building

3:10:09 needs some rethinking, but um

3:10:13 what um

3:10:15 we have discovered in that is that

3:10:18 now we have real points of contact that

3:10:21 have real meaning.

3:10:23 So, our advisors at those schools know

3:10:26 those 20 to 30 parents, depending on the

3:10:28 school, and can really be like, "Hey,

3:10:31 you know, Malcolm hasn't been doing his

3:10:32 history homework."

3:10:34 And can sit him down during win block

3:10:35 and be like, "Yeah,

3:10:37 you know, Dr. Myers is going to come and

3:10:38 work with you this day cuz you haven't

3:10:39 been doing your history homework."

3:10:41 You know, um and it's our first year, so

3:10:43 I need to evaluate on how to build and

3:10:45 how to reinforce and make sure I'm

3:10:47 evaluating and pushing. Um

3:10:51 Yeah, and I think, you know,

3:10:53 I think that's a real thing for us to

3:10:55 keep going as well as I think um

3:10:57 academically we're still doing pretty

3:10:58 well. I mean,

3:11:00 you know,

3:11:02 like I showed a picture of a girl who

3:11:03 graduated from Brown.

3:11:04 You know, we have two kids in the posse

3:11:05 program right now. We have like, you

3:11:07 know, you know,

3:11:09 yeah, [snorts] and this was a tough

3:11:10 group. Like, this was tough group this

3:11:11 year and still we're sending 80% to

3:11:13 college. So,

3:11:15 um

3:11:15 yeah, I think I think we're doing all

3:11:17 right. So, but we could always do better

3:11:19 and that's

3:11:20 that's clear.

3:11:21 Okay, so. Malcolm, thank you.

3:11:23 Thank you.

3:11:24 Appreciate your work.

3:11:25 THANK YOU.

3:11:27 [applause]

3:11:30 OKAY.

3:11:31 SOLAR PANEL installation in school

3:11:33 buildings presentation, Alexandria

3:11:35 Vecchio, and a possible vote to approve

3:11:38 those solar panels. Alexandria.

3:11:40 She's on Zoom right now.

3:11:42 She's on Zoom. Okay, great.

3:11:44 Hi, good evening everyone. Alexandra

3:11:47 Vecchio, Director of Sustainability and

3:11:48 Asset Resources for the Town of

3:11:50 Brookline. Thanks so much for your time

3:11:52 this evening.

3:11:53 Um I'm just going to pull up my slide

3:11:55 deck here. Give me 1 second.

3:12:11 Uh okay, great. Uh are folks able to see

3:12:13 my uh slide that says strategic solar

3:12:16 initiative?

3:12:17 Yes.

3:12:17 Yes, we can.

3:12:18 Okay, wonderful. Thank you. Um so as I

3:12:20 said, I'm Alexandra, I'm Director of

3:12:22 Sustainability and Asset Resources for

3:12:24 the town. Um our Deputy Director Shelley

3:12:26 Dean couldn't be with me this evening,

3:12:27 but she's been working extensively on

3:12:29 this and I think has

3:12:30 spoken to a couple of you prior to this

3:12:32 meeting. Um so uh appreciate everybody's

3:12:35 time uh leading up to the the meeting on

3:12:38 this topic.

3:12:39 So this evening, um I want to focus

3:12:41 specifically on the school projects cuz

3:12:42 obviously that's what's of most interest

3:12:44 to you, but I am just going to provide a

3:12:45 little bit of background and context

3:12:46 first because I think that's important

3:12:48 to understand

3:12:50 um what this is uh kind of nestled

3:12:52 within and a bit of the context around

3:12:53 what we're doing here.

3:12:56 So in Brookline, uh we have numerous

3:12:59 municipal solar projects um

3:13:01 currently existing and under

3:13:03 construction. So right now we have two

3:13:05 town-owned systems that are quite small

3:13:08 and and a bit older on the library as

3:13:11 well as the health building. And then we

3:13:13 have one town-owned system that's under

3:13:14 construction, very near completion at

3:13:17 the Driscoll School. Um I'm very excited

3:13:19 about that. I was before all of you um

3:13:22 last year, or maybe it was 2024. Um but

3:13:27 on the Driscoll uh solar project. Um and

3:13:30 then we have five different arrays that

3:13:32 are developed as power purchase

3:13:34 agreements, which essentially means that

3:13:35 we've leased out our rooftop um to a

3:13:38 solar developer. So, we're getting a

3:13:40 reduction on our energy bills, but we

3:13:41 don't um receive all of the savings from

3:13:44 that system.

3:13:45 So, uh right now we have um some

3:13:48 proposed projects that are coming up. Uh

3:13:50 one at Pierce School and then four other

3:13:52 sites in town uh for near-term

3:13:54 construction that are town-owned. And

3:13:57 then we're looking at some medium and

3:13:58 longer-term projects and anticipate town

3:14:01 ownership for those, but we'll have to

3:14:02 look at the finances as we move forward.

3:14:05 And really this is uh all coming from uh

3:14:09 over the past year a solar financial

3:14:11 working group was formed and included

3:14:13 members of the select board, of the Zero

3:14:15 Mission Advisory Board, the Expenditures

3:14:17 and Revenues Study Committee,

3:14:19 Commissioner of Public Works, uh

3:14:21 Commissioner Shute, and myself. Uh and

3:14:23 we were looking at where are the

3:14:25 opportunities for us to expand solar in

3:14:27 town and hopefully help increase energy

3:14:30 independence and reduce uh energy and

3:14:33 electricity bills uh

3:14:35 across across town and school

3:14:36 properties. And so, because of a federal

3:14:40 tax credit that exists but is going away

3:14:43 soon, uh we were looking at how can we

3:14:46 take advantage of those savings in the

3:14:48 short term and also build out uh a

3:14:51 longer term term revenue model as well.

3:14:54 Keeping in mind that we have to do all

3:14:56 of that in partnership uh and

3:14:58 coordination with any of our capital

3:14:59 construction projects. You know, we're

3:15:01 not going to add solar panels on top of

3:15:03 a roof that's scheduled to get repaired

3:15:06 or undergo significant um renovation in

3:15:09 the next 5 years. That doesn't make

3:15:10 sense. Um you know, your average uh

3:15:13 solar panel lifespan is well over 20

3:15:15 years. Uh typically they say 25. Um and

3:15:19 so, we want to make sure that we aren't

3:15:20 installing something that then is going

3:15:22 to have to be picked back up again. So,

3:15:24 being conscious of that in the context

3:15:26 of our larger building improvements.

3:15:29 So, the solar financial working group

3:15:30 came up with um a recommendation that

3:15:33 was uh step one, take advantage of the

3:15:36 federal investment tax credit. You'll

3:15:38 hear me refer to it as the ITC

3:15:41 this evening and

3:15:42 that is the tax credit that's

3:15:44 expiring. That's one that we were

3:15:46 working towards capturing for the

3:15:48 Driscoll project. And and now

3:15:52 we are trying to capture here in the

3:15:54 next few weeks actually. So for a

3:15:57 project to qualify

3:15:59 receive

3:16:01 up to 40% but typically around 30% of a

3:16:04 return on the project. You have to

3:16:06 commence construction before July 4th of

3:16:09 this year. So a few weeks from now and

3:16:12 then you have four years to place that

3:16:14 into service

3:16:16 or you can place it into service anytime

3:16:19 in 26 or 27 and start construction

3:16:23 within there and then complete it by the

3:16:25 December 31st 2027. That's a short time

3:16:27 frame especially when placed into

3:16:29 service means we have to have an

3:16:31 interconnection that occurs with the

3:16:33 utility Eversource.

3:16:35 And everyone is racing towards this

3:16:37 deadline because everybody wants to you

3:16:39 know see receive 30 to

3:16:42 40% back on their projects. And so

3:16:45 we are trying to give ourselves a little

3:16:47 bit more bandwidth

3:16:49 and make sure that we have commenced

3:16:51 construction by July 4th.

3:16:53 Commence construction doesn't actually

3:16:55 mean that we need to have you know

3:16:58 solar panels on the roofs. It just means

3:17:00 that we need to have established a down

3:17:02 payment of at least 5% for the project

3:17:05 cost and then we need to ensure that we

3:17:09 have a signed agreement for the purchase

3:17:12 of

3:17:13 said solar panels by that time.

3:17:17 So we're referring to this as a

3:17:19 two-track approach. Track one is really

3:17:20 to capture that ITC credit

3:17:24 and uh by July 4th and then track two

3:17:27 was to look at these longer-term sites,

3:17:30 um and we're currently advertising a

3:17:32 request for qualifications, an RFQ, um

3:17:35 with responses due at the end of August

3:17:37 um for us to find a solar vendor who

3:17:39 would be assessing all of our remaining

3:17:41 properties um and looking at site

3:17:43 feasibility and financial um analysis

3:17:45 for those. So, for the purposes of this

3:17:47 evening, I'm just focusing really on

3:17:49 track one and actually really just

3:17:50 focusing on the the school properties um

3:17:53 cuz I know those are most interest, but

3:17:55 just wanted folks to understand that

3:17:57 this is a piece of a much larger puzzle

3:17:59 that we're working on.

3:18:02 So, the uh solar opportunities that

3:18:05 we're looking at right now, one at

3:18:06 Pierce School, um which you'll see in

3:18:08 the top row there, and then we're

3:18:10 looking at Fire Station 1, the Main

3:18:12 Library, um

3:18:14 Heath School, and Brookline High School.

3:18:15 We're referring to it as Phase 2.

3:18:17 Brookline High School actually already

3:18:18 does have solar panels on part of the

3:18:20 building, so we would be adding on to

3:18:22 that. Um the ones that are there

3:18:23 currently are not owned by the town,

3:18:25 those are uh part of a leasing

3:18:27 structure. Um and so, for the purposes

3:18:30 of the Pierce School project, uh the

3:18:33 preliminary construction costs we're

3:18:34 looking at is $1.13 million.

3:18:38 Um that would be coming from the Pierce

3:18:40 project budget, um and that has been

3:18:43 reviewed and voted on by the Building

3:18:46 Commission.

3:18:47 Um the other projects, uh

3:18:50 particularly

3:18:52 um well, all of the other four, but in

3:18:55 for your interest, the Heath School and

3:18:56 Brookline High School, those dollars

3:18:58 would be coming from um a CIP item that

3:19:01 has been voted on through town meeting

3:19:04 um just this past month. Um and so,

3:19:07 that's a uh $3.2 million

3:19:10 allocation that is to go towards these

3:19:13 projects and then future solar projects

3:19:15 as well. So, um none of the dollars for

3:19:19 this capital project would be coming out

3:19:21 of um the schools budget.

3:19:25 Uh with the Well, I'll go walk through

3:19:27 each of these, but um the anticipated

3:19:30 cost for the high school is 688,000

3:19:33 and Brookline High School for the second

3:19:36 phase of solar is 878,000.

3:19:39 But looking for both of those just to

3:19:40 keep in context, Pierce School, that's a

3:19:42 325

3:19:43 kW system, and Hay School's 200

3:19:47 Brookline High School's a 261. Those are

3:19:50 pretty large systems. Driscoll School,

3:19:52 for example, is right around a 200 kW

3:19:54 system, and that's the the most recent

3:19:56 one that we've installed.

3:19:59 So, in the case of Pierce, there are

3:20:02 some contingencies that we're holding

3:20:03 with this. Again, just trying to

3:20:07 comply with what is necessary for that

3:20:09 July 4th deadline to be able to capture

3:20:12 the ITC. And so, we are looking at

3:20:15 a preliminary cost of 1.13 million. That

3:20:18 includes an allocation

3:20:21 of

3:20:22 some contingency for both roofing over

3:20:25 the mechanical systems where we would

3:20:28 like to install solar there as well to

3:20:30 take advantage of the additional space

3:20:33 up there. And then a contingency of

3:20:35 50,000 for the racking system that we

3:20:38 use to connect the solar to the roof.

3:20:40 And then an overall construction

3:20:41 contingency. So, at the end, this

3:20:43 project may actually end up being less

3:20:45 than 1.1

3:20:47 million, but we are

3:20:49 going to make sure we put enough of a

3:20:52 down payment to satisfy that ITC

3:20:55 requirement. And in this case, the

3:20:57 payback timeline for Pierce

3:21:00 would actually be within less than a

3:21:02 year after you take into account the

3:21:04 state incentives that we're able to get

3:21:06 off this project as well as the

3:21:11 savings from the ITC. We're looking at

3:21:16 um

3:21:17 positive and then the savings towards

3:21:19 our electric bills we're actually

3:21:20 looking at um a system that is uh net

3:21:23 flow positive um in our first year,

3:21:25 which is

3:21:26 pretty extraordinary.

3:21:29 Uh for Hayes School, um a 200 uh kW

3:21:32 system with a preliminary cost of

3:21:34 688,000,

3:21:36 and um we're looking at a payback

3:21:38 timeline of just over 6 years. Um right

3:21:42 now for each of these because we don't

3:21:44 haven't done a final structural

3:21:45 analysis, um we are carrying a 5%

3:21:47 contingency, but again, um should any of

3:21:51 these systems be sized down or um the

3:21:54 cost uh end up being lower, that would

3:21:57 be um reflected in the actual uh

3:22:00 contracts and invoices that we receive.

3:22:04 Uh so the cash flow projections for

3:22:06 Hayes School, um as you can see once we

3:22:08 take into account the state incentive

3:22:10 revenue as well as um after the invest-

3:22:14 investment tax credit uh return of

3:22:16 481,000,

3:22:18 um after about 6 years we're looking at

3:22:21 um a system that is uh

3:22:24 uh

3:22:25 uh net flow net cash flow positive, uh

3:22:28 which is fantastic. Um and again, really

3:22:31 good numbers. We uh look at the savings

3:22:33 over 20-year period, but most systems

3:22:35 actually last last longer than that. Um

3:22:38 and the cost we do carry in O&M cost,

3:22:41 that's what that um

3:22:42 uh O&M column is um that you can see on

3:22:45 the screen. Um starting in year two, the

3:22:48 first year O&M is covered through the

3:22:50 installation contract.

3:22:53 And then finally, Brookline High School,

3:22:55 um as you can see we already have um

3:22:57 solar panels on one part of the

3:22:59 building, so this would be adding um

3:23:01 remaining panels in the remaining areas,

3:23:04 um which would be an additional 261 uh

3:23:07 kW system at a preliminary cost of

3:23:10 878,000,

3:23:12 and a payback timeline of just around 6

3:23:14 years. Again, that's a that's a really

3:23:16 promising timeline. We wouldn't be

3:23:18 looking at those kind of numbers if it

3:23:20 wasn't for us being able to get 30% back

3:23:22 off of each of these projects.

3:23:25 Um so again here you can see

3:23:28 some electricity bill savings as the

3:23:30 system is up and running from day one,

3:23:33 but then when you take into account the

3:23:35 state incentives

3:23:36 and the investment tax credit um

3:23:40 um we're looking at um

3:23:43 being

3:23:44 net cash flow positive in year seven.

3:23:48 So

3:23:49 I

3:23:50 for today's purposes I wanted to one

3:23:53 make sure you all were aware of this

3:23:55 initiative. I think these are the first

3:23:57 three schools that we're looking at, but

3:23:59 I anticipate over the coming years as we

3:24:01 look at those track two projects we'll

3:24:03 have others that are coming forward. Um

3:24:06 and as I said the intention is for us to

3:24:10 use the

3:24:12 CIP funds, the capital improvement

3:24:14 funds,

3:24:15 for funding these projects with the

3:24:16 exception of Pierce since solar was

3:24:19 included in that budget. Um but for

3:24:22 today's purposes I am hoping to answer

3:24:25 any questions and discuss the projects

3:24:27 with you to the extent you're

3:24:28 interested, but

3:24:30 looking for a vote of approval for

3:24:34 the solar

3:24:36 at Pierce, Franklin and Hayes school and

3:24:39 then the designation of Superintendent

3:24:42 Wong or whomever you see most

3:24:44 appropriate with the authority to sign

3:24:46 any project contract documents on behalf

3:24:49 of the school committee just because

3:24:50 we're going to be moving really quickly

3:24:52 over the next couple of weeks from

3:24:55 approving these final project numbers to

3:24:57 actually award and executing a contract

3:24:59 so that we can comply with that July 4th

3:25:01 deadline. And all of this would be

3:25:03 reviewed and approved by town council's

3:25:05 office um

3:25:08 Um so with that, I will uh stop sharing

3:25:11 and am happy to take um any question.

3:25:15 Thank you, Alexandra. This Let's Let's

3:25:18 make a motion.

3:28:07 Okay question do we move to the next

3:28:09 agenda item or take public comment at

3:28:13 Let's go [clears throat] public comment

3:28:15 who's next I don't have here we go.

3:28:19 We have

3:28:20 Rosen.

3:28:22 Rosen Valchev. [clears throat]

3:28:24 Oh great. Thank you for hanging out.

3:28:29 Hope you're eating a lot of the food.

3:28:33 Thanks.

3:28:35 All right

3:28:36 well I'm here to

3:28:38 give a public comment about the section

3:28:39 reductions as well

3:28:41 and

3:28:42 I think they raised a number of issues

3:28:44 the ones I want to speak about are

3:28:45 equity but also fiscal responsibility.

3:28:48 So I first I want to start off by saying

3:28:49 that I think the superintendent is being

3:28:51 very prudent in observing that we have a

3:28:52 low K enrollment overall in the district

3:28:55 and probably makes sense to move

3:28:57 resources to other priorities, maybe

3:28:59 medical, maybe the

3:29:00 materials and fees program, but what I'm

3:29:03 puzzled by is why, given that we're

3:29:05 going to reduce one case section, why

3:29:07 we're reducing it at Hiram School and

3:29:09 not at the low enrollment school. So,

3:29:12 I think we've all seen these numbers,

3:29:13 but reducing it at the high enrollment

3:29:15 school of FRR means the class sizes go

3:29:17 to 19, 20, while some other class sizes

3:29:20 are around the 12.

3:29:23 Oh, I see. Now now I see it. So,

3:29:26 anyways, by cutting at the high

3:29:27 enrollment school, we have class sizes

3:29:29 19, 20, while other schools have class

3:29:30 sizes of 12. That's a 50% difference.

3:29:33 Now,

3:29:34 if that was the only option to cut, then

3:29:36 that makes perfect sense, but there's

3:29:38 other options to cut other places with

3:29:39 low enrollment schools, and in those

3:29:41 options, we get balanced class sizes of

3:29:43 15 or 16 throughout throughout the

3:29:45 board. So, I'm puzzled why we're going

3:29:48 with the unbalanced option, and going

3:29:49 with the unbalanced option means two

3:29:51 things at least. One is equity, and a

3:29:53 number of people already spoke about

3:29:54 equity. So, I don't want to repeat it,

3:29:56 but I think it's an important point. I

3:29:58 do want to point out also that FRR is a

3:30:00 Title 1 school, and it's losing not only

3:30:03 the kindergarten section, but also the

3:30:04 third grade section. So, there is a

3:30:06 number of questions here why that the

3:30:08 Title 1 school is landing a lot of cuts.

3:30:11 Again, this this speaks about equity. A

3:30:13 number of people have already pointed

3:30:14 this out to the committee, so I want to

3:30:16 move on to speaking about the fiscal

3:30:18 responsibility, which I haven't heard

3:30:19 too much about. So, it makes sense to

3:30:22 reduce resources in low enrollment

3:30:24 grades, like, "Okay, move it somewhere

3:30:26 else."

3:30:27 Um but at the same time, I don't

3:30:28 understand why don't we do it in a

3:30:30 balanced way, because somewhere

3:30:31 enrollment is coming. As we heard from

3:30:33 the guidance counselor, there's a lot of

3:30:35 kids enrolling in the summer, especially

3:30:37 at FRR. So, if we start FRR in the

3:30:40 beginning of summer at like 19, 20, the

3:30:42 likelihood of moving above 22, which

3:30:44 I've heard several times being mentioned

3:30:46 as the guidance as the guidance number,

3:30:48 is is that, you know, a significant

3:30:50 number. So, if

3:30:52 FRR doesn't move, I'm sorry.

3:30:55 If FRR does move above 22, but again,

3:30:58 this summer was going to happen. Most

3:30:59 likely district is going to move a K

3:31:00 section back to FRR. And then what what

3:31:03 does that mean? That means that at the

3:31:04 end of the day we didn't save the

3:31:05 budgetary numbers we were looking to

3:31:06 save anyways, right? So, the fiscally

3:31:09 prudent thing to do is to start

3:31:11 everybody at 15 16. Then there's no

3:31:13 enrollment risk. It's unlikely we're

3:31:15 going to have to bring a K section back

3:31:17 anywhere. And we actually managed to

3:31:19 save some resources to put towards other

3:31:21 priorities.

3:31:22 So, um at the end of the day I think

3:31:24 having an unbalanced plan must make

3:31:26 sense according to some factors. I don't

3:31:29 know what exactly those factors are, but

3:31:30 I want to bring it to the community's

3:31:31 attention that there are also equity and

3:31:34 fiscal responsibility issues at hand.

3:31:36 Thank you.

3:31:40 Mike Lyons.

3:31:43 Mike Lyons, thank you.

3:31:56 Hi. My name is Mike Lyons. I have one

3:31:59 FRR student in the first grade. I have

3:32:01 another starting in kindergarten this

3:32:03 fall.

3:32:04 There's two points I'm hoping to

3:32:06 convince you of tonight in addition to

3:32:08 what other folks have said.

3:32:10 First, that I would like to um

3:32:14 evaluate class sizes

3:32:16 by what each student experiences.

3:32:19 Not just looking at, you know, a

3:32:21 zero-sum school versus school

3:32:24 um thing. Second, I'm hoping we can

3:32:26 publish registration data before making

3:32:29 cuts,

3:32:30 both historical and each year going

3:32:31 forward, the same way we already publish

3:32:34 the attendance data of the students who

3:32:35 actually start in September.

3:32:38 So, if you wouldn't mind looking at

3:32:39 slide one, I'm going to roll through

3:32:40 these pretty quick cuz I have 3 minutes.

3:32:42 Um

3:32:44 there's a school-by-school registration

3:32:46 data from the last meeting. I know it's

3:32:47 changed a little bit. I looked over the

3:32:49 I recomputed it on my computer. I'm

3:32:51 happy to show you later. Um

3:32:54 it the trends still hold. Um it's

3:32:56 lopsided and you know, if a cut is

3:32:58 needed as others have said, there are

3:33:00 more balanced places to do it.

3:33:02 Um but if you go to slide two,

3:33:04 and I I know I said I want don't want

3:33:06 this to be school versus school.

3:33:08 Uh this chart shows what each incoming K

3:33:12 student experiences.

3:33:15 So, there's 380 bars there. Each bar is

3:33:18 one student coming in and the height of

3:33:20 that bar

3:33:21 is the size of that class that student

3:33:24 experiences.

3:33:26 So, as you can see, cutting an FRR

3:33:27 section creates a wide range of class

3:33:30 sizes across

3:33:31 the district.

3:33:33 And you can see it also affects an

3:33:34 outsized number of students. You can see

3:33:36 that bar at the very end representing

3:33:38 FRR is very wide.

3:33:41 Um that's because FRR has the largest

3:33:44 set of K classes of any school.

3:33:47 So, you'd also expect the average class

3:33:49 size and the average size experienced by

3:33:52 students to match.

3:33:54 That's those two different lines you see

3:33:55 there.

3:33:56 But you'll notice those lines are

3:33:57 actually

3:33:58 there is a gap.

3:34:00 And that gap is because there's so many

3:34:03 FRR students that will experience a

3:34:04 larger class

3:34:06 than the other students in the district.

3:34:10 So, closing that gap, I think would make

3:34:12 the experience across the district far

3:34:15 more even, fair, and equitable

3:34:17 regardless of what school

3:34:20 each child attends.

3:34:22 Um but given the questions about late

3:34:24 arrivals,

3:34:25 I I also modeled two scenarios.

3:34:28 Uh flat attendance and if the past 3

3:34:31 years trends continue.

3:34:32 Uh so, if you look at slide eight,

3:34:34 uh with flat attendance,

3:34:36 which may or may not happen,

3:34:39 um

3:34:40 the year's actual numbers are pretty

3:34:41 even.

3:34:43 This year's numbers, but cutting one

3:34:45 section creates a bigger gap than

3:34:46 before.

3:34:47 So, FRR students become big outliers and

3:34:50 above the 22 student per class guidance.

3:34:54 If you look at slide 11,

3:34:57 instead of saying flat attendance, it

3:34:59 says what if we follow the trends that

3:35:00 have happened over the past few years.

3:35:02 You'll notice FRR attendance grows

3:35:04 slightly and climbs to 23.5 students per

3:35:07 class,

3:35:08 but

3:35:10 no matter how you slice it, if you cut

3:35:12 any schools one section at any school,

3:35:16 you cannot get it under 22.

3:35:19 So, my point here is not we should cut

3:35:22 this school or this school. My point

3:35:23 here is that without the data that says

3:35:26 how the registrations

3:35:30 lead to what who actually attends,

3:35:33 it's very very difficult, if not

3:35:35 impossible, to accurately predict who

3:35:37 will be here in September.

3:35:39 And so, kind of my second point is that

3:35:41 publishing that data, which

3:35:44 I believe the district already has

3:35:46 and hopefully is factoring in, I think

3:35:48 would help everybody not just know when

3:35:50 to push back,

3:35:51 but when not to push back.

3:35:54 So, to close, I hope we can consider

3:35:56 that student level impact

3:35:58 and two that we can publish the

3:36:00 registration data in addition to the

3:36:02 attendance data going forward. Thanks.

3:36:04 Thank you, Mike.

3:36:10 [applause]

3:36:12 Next up is Irit.

3:36:16 She's on Zoom. Irit Moore.

3:36:22 Yes, can you hear me?

3:36:25 Yes, we can.

3:36:26 Okay, perfect.

3:36:28 Great. So, good evening, everyone. My

3:36:31 name is Irit Moore.

3:36:33 I'm an FRR parent. My oldest daughter is

3:36:37 currently at FRR and my youngest son is

3:36:39 entering kindergarten next year.

3:36:42 Um I know it's been a long meeting. Um I

3:36:44 also know that our wonderful community

3:36:47 has already sent many emails with

3:36:49 questions and concerns about the planned

3:36:52 reduction

3:36:53 um of one kindergarten section.

3:36:55 Um earlier tonight you heard the numbers

3:36:58 even out.

3:36:59 Um you heard from Jordana about what

3:37:01 this looks like on the ground.

3:37:03 Um who the children are, what happens

3:37:05 over the summer, and what it means to

3:37:07 make this cut now, and then potentially

3:37:10 need to rebuild capacity later.

3:37:13 I don't want to repeat all of that.

3:37:15 Instead, I want to add the perspective

3:37:17 of a parent.

3:37:19 Three years ago, my older daughter

3:37:21 entered kindergarten at FRR shortly

3:37:24 after we moved here from Israel.

3:37:26 She arrived with literally no English.

3:37:29 Um for some children, the transition to

3:37:32 kindergarten is smooth. For others, it's

3:37:34 much harder.

3:37:36 For children who are new to the

3:37:37 language, new to the country, or simply

3:37:40 quiet and unsure, it can be a very very

3:37:44 big transition.

3:37:46 I saw firsthand how important it is for

3:37:48 a child to be seen,

3:37:50 not just taught, seen.

3:37:53 I saw how much it matters when a teacher

3:37:55 has the time and space to notice the

3:37:58 child who is quiet,

3:38:00 to understand who needs help,

3:38:02 to build trust, to create a classroom

3:38:05 where children feel safe enough to try,

3:38:08 to speak, to make mistakes, and to grow.

3:38:12 That does that that does not happen by

3:38:14 accident. It happens because we have

3:38:16 excellent teachers and administrators.

3:38:19 But even excellent teachers need the

3:38:21 right conditions to do their work well.

3:38:24 Smaller and balanced classrooms,

3:38:27 real support, and enough capacity to

3:38:29 meet the children where they where they

3:38:31 are.

3:38:33 And now, 3 years later,

3:38:34 I'm preparing to send my younger kid

3:38:36 into kindergarten.

3:38:38 Last week, we had the peek and practice

3:38:40 where incoming kindergartens came to

3:38:42 visit school.

3:38:44 For some of them, it was their first

3:38:45 experience and first time walking into

3:38:47 this building.

3:38:49 I took a moment to look at the children.

3:38:52 Some were excited,

3:38:54 some and some were really ready for the

3:38:56 next step,

3:38:57 but some were nervous. Some looked

3:38:59 terrified.

3:39:01 And honestly, it makes sense. FRR is a

3:39:03 very, very big uh school, big building,

3:39:06 especially compared to the small

3:39:07 preschool experience and environment

3:39:10 uh many of them are coming from.

3:39:13 You know, at the end of the day,

3:39:15 these are tiny 5-year-old kids.

3:39:18 It's our responsibility to make sure

3:39:20 their first experience entering public

3:39:23 school system is a strong, supported

3:39:26 supportive, and positive as it can be.

3:39:29 I understand that the district has

3:39:31 difficult choices to make,

3:39:33 but I trust the creativity and the

3:39:35 leadership of the administration to find

3:39:37 other solutions if cuts are needed, not

3:39:40 by taking capacity away from our

3:39:42 youngest learners at the very beginning

3:39:44 of their school experience.

3:39:47 I want to be able to look at my son the

3:39:49 night before his first day of

3:39:50 kindergarten,

3:39:52 hug him tight, and tell him with full

3:39:54 confidence that he's going to have a

3:39:56 wonderful school experience

3:39:59 because we chose the best place for him.

3:40:02 And because the adults responsible for

3:40:04 that place made the right decision for

3:40:06 him and for his classmates.

3:40:09 So, I'm asking you, please keep the

3:40:11 fifth kindergarten section at FRR.

3:40:13 Thank you.

3:40:15 Toda, Elit.

3:40:17 Um

3:40:21 Chichi Wu.

3:40:22 Uh she's withdrawn.

3:40:24 Withdrawn.

3:40:25 Uh Deborah Brown.

3:40:29 I did.

3:40:31 No?

3:40:33 Uh, Zariana?

3:40:41 Thanks for hanging in there.

3:40:43 Um, hi everyone. My name is Zariana

3:40:45 Katicich and Jones and Malcolm Cawthorne

3:40:47 asked me to speak here tonight on behalf

3:40:49 of METCO and in support of METCO.

3:40:51 Over the past several months I have

3:40:53 heard a growing conversation about the

3:40:54 cost of the METCO program here in

3:40:57 Brookline. While I understand that

3:40:58 budgets matter, I believe we are asking

3:41:00 the wrong question. The question should

3:41:02 not be how much does METCO cost? The

3:41:04 question should be what is the value of

3:41:06 the community we are building here

3:41:07 together.

3:41:08 As someone who grew up in Brookline, I

3:41:10 can tell you firsthand that racial

3:41:11 diversity is not an abstract concept. It

3:41:14 is not a statistic in a report. It is a

3:41:16 difference between children growing up

3:41:17 in isolation and children growing up

3:41:19 learning from one another.

3:41:21 It is a difference between a school

3:41:22 system that simply talks about inclusion

3:41:24 and one that actively practices it.

3:41:27 For decades, METCO has brought students

3:41:29 and families into Brookline who have

3:41:31 enriched our schools, challenged us to

3:41:32 think more broadly, and helped create

3:41:34 the diverse learning environment that so

3:41:36 many people in this town claim to value.

3:41:39 As a Brookline student of color, I know

3:41:40 how important it was to learn alongside

3:41:42 students from different backgrounds and

3:41:44 experiences. METCO did not just benefit

3:41:47 the students who participated in the

3:41:48 program. It benefited us all.

3:41:51 This is why I find some of the recent

3:41:52 public conversations surrounding METCO

3:41:54 deeply concerning. At a time when

3:41:56 diversity, equity, and inclusion

3:41:58 initiatives are facing attacks across

3:42:00 the country, including from the Trump

3:42:01 administration, Brookline continues to

3:42:03 publicly state that these values matter

3:42:05 to us. Yet, many of our members of our

3:42:07 community have watched as

3:42:08 diversity-focused initiatives have

3:42:10 increasingly become targets for

3:42:11 criticism, including the vote to

3:42:13 eliminate the Office of Educational

3:42:15 Equity and ongoing conversations

3:42:16 questioning programs like METCO.

3:42:18 Whether intentional or not, these

3:42:20 actions create the perception that

3:42:21 diversity, equity, and inclusion efforts

3:42:23 are being treated as optional rather

3:42:25 than essential. For many black and brown

3:42:27 families, that perception matters.

3:42:29 Before I close, I also want to briefly

3:42:31 acknowledge an email that I and several

3:42:33 others received from Sarah. She shared

3:42:35 that tonight's presentation is intended

3:42:37 to celebrate METCO, not to debate its

3:42:39 budget. I agree wholeheartedly that

3:42:41 Malcolm Cawthorn, the students,

3:42:43 families, and staff who make this

3:42:45 program possible deserve to have their

3:42:46 accomplishments recognized and

3:42:48 celebrated. At the same time, I think it

3:42:50 would be disingenuous to ignore why so

3:42:51 many people have felt compelled to speak

3:42:53 up recently. Celebrating METCO and

3:42:55 protecting its future are not

3:42:56 contradictory goals. In fact, they go

3:42:58 hand in hand. It is important that our

3:43:00 community understands that programs like

3:43:02 METCO should not become convenient

3:43:03 targets whenever difficult financial

3:43:05 conversations arise. If we truly value

3:43:07 METCO what METCO students contribute to

3:43:09 Brookline, then we must be willing to

3:43:10 support it not only during celebrations,

3:43:13 but also when decisions are made about

3:43:14 priorities, investments, and resources.

3:43:16 Because ultimately, METCO is not just a

3:43:18 line item in a budget. It is a statement

3:43:20 about who we are as a community. It is a

3:43:22 commitment to educational opportunity,

3:43:24 racial understanding, and the belief

3:43:25 that our children are stronger when they

3:43:26 learn together. So tonight, I celebrate

3:43:28 METCO. I celebrate its students, its

3:43:30 families, its staff, and its impact. And

3:43:32 I urge this community to continue

3:43:33 protecting, supporting, and investing in

3:43:35 the program that has helped make

3:43:36 Brookline a stronger, more diverse, and

3:43:38 more compassionate place for

3:43:40 generations. Also, shameless plug,

3:43:42 tomorrow's Juneteenth. Please make sure

3:43:43 you come out. It's going to be at

3:43:44 Cypress Field at the high school. So,

3:43:46 thank you very much. Have a great night.

3:43:48 Thank you, Zarianna.

3:43:49 [applause]

3:43:52 Angela Peoples.

3:44:04 Peoples.

3:45:35 child um um

3:45:37 harassed my child,

3:45:51 It's so bad that um

3:45:53 the other student wanted to pay another

3:46:00 um

3:46:01 while y'all sitting here and you know,

3:46:03 hate to spoil the celebration, but my

3:46:06 child education is being affected and so

3:46:08 is my living here

3:46:10 at this time and being involved.

3:46:13 I'm an advocate for parents on many

3:46:15 levels. I like to help people, but right

3:46:18 now I need help.

3:46:20 So, I also want to talk about the fact

3:46:22 that um

3:46:25 I am concerned for the other child and

3:46:27 the other There were several children

3:46:28 involved.

3:46:30 Um so,

3:46:32 the mind of our children is being

3:46:34 affected and if you think what's

3:46:36 happening to me is not you you you're

3:46:38 not bothered, guess what? You are

3:46:40 bothered by this because at the same

3:46:42 time when you're home resting, what

3:46:44 affects someone's social and emotional

3:46:47 can it it's traveling into the schools.

3:46:50 And you may not see us on the news like

3:46:53 things happening in Boston, Brookline,

3:46:55 and things like that, but you got to

3:46:57 understand this is where it starts. So,

3:46:59 something's being given birth to the

3:47:02 child that has harassed my child to feel

3:47:04 the way that he's feels. So, I also

3:47:07 have prayed for that child and his

3:47:09 family and I feel bad about it.

3:47:11 And that's why I'm here tonight to tell

3:47:13 you guys that um that's very important,

3:47:16 the social and emotional aspect of

3:47:17 dealing with children.

3:47:19 And um it cannot be ignored.

3:47:23 If you don't deal with these things

3:47:24 today, you will deal with them in the

3:47:26 future.

3:47:28 The Bible even says that words are life

3:47:30 and death. And so, the way that we talk

3:47:33 to each other is very important. If I

3:47:36 encourage you today, I'm giving life to

3:47:38 you. If I insult you, I'm making you

3:47:40 feel bad, but a lot of people, you know,

3:47:43 my children can be resilient, but this

3:47:45 really affected my child and I want to

3:47:48 say I'm here to stand for my child and

3:47:51 speak for my child. And that we as a

3:47:54 community and also a committee and all

3:47:56 of that, we have to work at what our

3:47:59 children are saying. Hear your child,

3:48:01 listen to your child, believe your

3:48:03 child,

3:48:04 and take time to

3:48:07 be with them during the day. And then

3:48:09 you'll know what's going on. And I want

3:48:11 to say I'm very upset that I emailed

3:48:13 you, Superintendent Bellwong, and I got

3:48:15 no response. So now I see how it goes.

3:48:18 I wasn't getting a response from the

3:48:20 school, and I didn't get a response from

3:48:21 you. Somebody will be speaking to me.

3:48:24 Thank you.

3:48:26 Thank you, Angela.

3:48:30 Germaine.

3:48:37 Hello, my name is

3:48:39 Oh. Hello, my name is Germaine

3:48:42 Hello, my name is Germaine Northcross.

3:48:48 I'm a mom

3:49:14 One One guy followed my son in the

3:49:24 to bang anything?

3:49:40 divided we fall.

3:49:47 um

3:49:48 33 years old. She graduated from Salem

3:49:50 State. She went to to the program

3:49:53 Juniper,

3:49:54 thanks to Medco program,

3:49:57 thanks to Jean Maguire, who gave her a

3:49:59 chance.

3:50:00 Um she's my second child that I

3:50:03 gave birth to.

3:50:04 The placenta broke five different

3:50:08 became a journalist. Um, she's a mom.

3:50:12 My first child, Ashley, she went to

3:50:14 Stitch it.

3:50:16 Again,

3:50:18 Brookline,

3:50:20 Medco first gave me a chance, gave my

3:50:22 family a chance to come out here and see

3:50:26 anyone other than

3:50:29 someone that look my like me. I mean, I

3:50:31 I now I I love diversity. I said, "Yeah,

3:50:35 this is why I named all my kids colors."

3:50:37 Um,

3:50:38 um, I just

3:50:40 enough of this. Enough of this racism,

3:50:48 Um, work together as a community, work

3:50:51 together as

3:50:52 human beings. I don't I haven't taught

3:51:15 divided we fall. What are we standing

3:51:17 for?

3:51:19 What are we standing for?

3:54:29 And, um, so I created a document. I'm

3:54:31 not going to review that document at

3:54:35 reflection of the end-of-year report,

3:54:37 but it also where we are against the

3:54:42 2024-2027

3:54:53 look at the goals proposed, that, um,

3:54:57 whereas coming into this year, our large

3:54:59 focus was looking at our financial

3:55:01 systems, you'll see that I have shifted

3:55:03 to other goals.

3:55:05 Um, the DESE model says

3:55:08 that there must be one student learning

3:55:10 goal,

3:55:11 one professional practice goal, and two

3:55:14 to four district improvement goals, and

3:55:17 I've chosen four.

3:55:19 All right. And then, when Pardon?

3:55:23 I don't know.

3:55:24 Just

3:55:25 So, um,

3:55:27 and then for each goal selected,

3:55:30 um, just to indicate where the focus

3:55:32 indicators are.

3:55:34 What, um,

3:55:36 Valerie shared with you was a document

3:55:38 with all the indicators, and so they're

3:55:41 just noted by

3:55:43 Roman numeral and section here, so you

3:55:45 can refer back to them.

3:55:47 Um so, for I'm proposing for the student

3:55:51 learning goal, and again, you're

3:55:53 supposed to pick things that are smart

3:55:55 goals, so sort of a distinct product or

3:55:59 outcome. So, the student learning goal

3:56:02 is to to decrease disproportionately for

3:56:05 PSB students so as to no longer be

3:56:07 identified by DESI.

3:56:09 District has been identified twice in

3:56:11 the last 3 years for disproportionality,

3:56:14 so that would be the student learning

3:56:16 goal.

3:56:17 The professional practice goal is to

3:56:20 achieve 100% evaluation of all unit A

3:56:24 and unit B and unaligned administrators

3:56:27 to sustain high-quality instruction. The

3:56:30 other part is to implement an evaluation

3:56:33 system for paraprofessionals.

3:56:35 So, at this time, there is no evaluation

3:56:38 document for paraprofessionals. So, the

3:56:40 goal would be to do that, and that has

3:56:42 actually been a subject of negotiations

3:56:45 at this time.

3:56:47 Um the strategic plan that was in play

3:56:51 or is in place 24 to 27 notes targets of

3:56:55 getting

3:56:56 um

3:56:57 75% evaluation, 90%, and 100%. I'm just

3:57:00 going to name the 100% as the target.

3:57:03 All right, so this is for unit A, unit

3:57:06 B, and unaligned administrators.

3:57:10 The district improvement goals

3:57:13 Some of this reflects the work that we

3:57:16 have committed to do as part of our

3:57:18 MOUs,

3:57:19 um but also makes sense for us to do as

3:57:22 a district. So, the first one you may

3:57:25 recognize, evaluate the grade

3:57:27 configurations to improve scheduling and

3:57:29 staff equity across the K-8 schools. All

3:57:33 right, so that would be one.

3:57:35 Um the second one is to establish an

3:57:39 equity leadership team and develop a

3:57:43 state. So, when we get to the

3:57:45 superintendent um report update, I will

3:57:49 also share the updated um expectations

3:57:53 that were communicated by the

3:57:59 identified. We don't have

3:58:05 have an equity plan. So, that would be

3:58:07 the specific outcome of that goal.

3:58:33 um for for us to focus on in the coming

4:28:59 there has been um

4:29:05 um over the last year and the year

4:29:16 And so, there has been much comment

4:29:18 about our

4:29:19 the um

4:29:21 average class size for K at FRR being

4:29:24 larger than um

4:29:27 the K averages at other schools.

4:29:29 So, I have to remind everyone that when

4:29:32 we built the budgets,

4:29:34 the budgets were based on guidance.

4:29:37 And so, that is our magic for for class

4:29:41 sizes as we move forward.

4:29:43 The K sections might be smaller at some

4:29:46 schools than others,

4:29:48 and um

4:29:49 it actually

4:29:51 this is something where we're

4:29:52 anticipating that we have to look and

4:29:54 see if we're adjusting when we look at

4:29:56 transition from K to 1.

4:29:59 Um having eight K-8 schools, some at two

4:30:02 sections, some are five section schools,

4:30:05 within any K-8 school, you have a range

4:30:08 of two to five sections across the grade

4:30:11 levels. It's really um it's been it's

4:30:15 nearly impossible for when you divide

4:30:17 the number across the schools for any

4:30:20 grade level that you won't have some

4:30:23 range.

4:30:24 Um we are committed to look at grade

4:30:27 configurations for schools um in the

4:30:30 next year.

4:30:32 Um we were largely looking at

4:30:34 possibility of middle school models or

4:30:37 not. It's not a commitment to go to that

4:30:40 model. It's that we have a commitment to

4:30:43 explore and see what that would do in

4:30:45 trying to even out the class sizes

4:30:48 across the district. The other

4:30:50 possibility looking at grade

4:30:52 configurations is redistricting.

4:30:55 So, that has also very disruptive impact

4:31:00 to the community. So, what I want to

4:31:03 emphasize with AK8 schools that are so

4:31:06 different in their number of sections

4:31:09 per school, when we divide, we are going

4:31:12 to have to expect that we're going to

4:31:15 have variability in the section sizes.

4:31:17 The metric we are using is making sure

4:31:21 we are we are

4:31:24 that we're adhering to class size

4:31:25 guidance. Right now, across all schools,

4:31:29 there is um

4:31:31 one grade level that looks like it's

4:31:34 it's at this moment

4:31:37 at and slightly above guidance, but all

4:31:40 other sections across the district are

4:31:42 well under guidance for those grade

4:31:45 levels. We will continue to monitor

4:31:48 through the summer, but trying to even

4:31:51 out the the sections

4:31:54 is a challenge for us. And so, we

4:31:56 understand that there is variability.

4:58:57 Last week, Friday.

4:58:59 Last week. Um, each year the Brookline

4:59:01 Special Education Parent Advisory

4:59:03 Council, also known as SEPAC, Brookline

4:59:06 SEPAC, recognizes the incredible

4:59:08 individuals who have made a meaningful

4:59:10 difference in the lives of our children.

4:59:12 These are our SEPAC heroes. They are

4:59:16 teachers who go the extra mile, the

4:59:18 paraprofessionals who offer steady and

4:59:20 caring support,

4:59:22 the bus drivers who greet our children

4:59:24 with a smile every day, the

4:59:25 administrators who listen and lead with

4:59:28 compassion,

4:59:29 and the PSB or out of district community

4:59:31 members who step up to ensure that every

4:59:33 child feels seen, supported, and

4:59:35 included.

4:59:37 Um, I think um, the very special thing

4:59:39 about SEPAC Hero Awards and um, Deb

4:59:41 Caruso, who was here earlier um,

4:59:45 being recognized for her 31 years of

4:59:47 service, um, mentioned that these this

4:59:50 as well, that the nominations are um,

4:59:54 unique because they come directly from

4:59:56 families who have taken the time to

4:59:58 share the many ways these individuals

5:00:00 have changed lives through kindness,

5:00:02 dedication, advocacy, creativity, and

5:00:04 heart. So, uh, we honor and celebrate

5:00:07 these exceptional people. They may not

5:00:09 seek recognition, but their work

5:00:10 deserves it. So, I want to take a moment

5:00:13 to read their names and thank and

5:00:15 recognize our 2026

5:00:18 SEPAC Heroes, and those are I'm going to

5:00:20 I'm probably not going to pronounce some
